summary: Deep adversarial correctness audit of the FileID WinUI 3 C#/XAML app (10 areas, refute-by-default verify)
logs: App audit: 20 raw; 16 CONFIRMED (>=2/3 real); 0 contested (1 real vote)

confirmed.title: ReadBoundedFrameAsync rescans the whole buffer per chunk — O(n²) CPU hang on a large restructurePlan frame
confirmed.description: The frame reader scans for the terminating newline by walking the ENTIRE StringBuilder from index 0 on every outer-loop iteration (the `for (int i = 0; i < st.Buffer.Length; i++)` at lines 592-595), and only then reads the next 16 KiB chunk (line 619) and appends it (line 632). For a single newline-delimited frame of size N built from N/16K reads, the total work is the sum of the buffer length over every chunk, i.e. O(N²/chunkSize) StringBuilder indexer accesses — and `StringBuilder`'s indexer is itself O(chunks) for a large multi-chunk builder, so the real cost is worse. This is exactly the happy path the cap was deliberately raised to 32 MiB to support: the comment at lines 56-64 states a full restructure plan for the product target (~200k moves, one move ≈ 300 B) must fit in one frame, and the engine writes each event as a single JSON line terminated by `\n` (engine ipc/sink.rs write_frame: serde_json::to_writer + write_all(b"\n")). So a real `restructurePlan` on a tens-of-thousands-to-200k-file library produces a ~10-32 MiB single line, and decoding it pegs the StdoutLoopAsync thread for many seconds (tens of billions of char comparisons) before the Restructure tab can populate — a visible hang on precisely the large-library scenario the product targets. Smaller events are unaffected, which is why it would pass small-corpus testing and only bite the 'tens of thousands of files' user.
confirmed.trigger: Run Plan Restructure on a large library (e.g. 50k+ files) so the engine emits a multi-megabyte single-line restructurePlan event; StdoutLoopAsync spins a CPU core for seconds-to-minutes re-scanning the growing buffer before the plan is decoded and the Restructure tab renders.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Track an incremental scan offset in StdoutFraming so each appended chunk's bytes are examined for '\n' at most once. E.g. add `public int Scanned;` to StdoutFraming, start the newline search at `st.Scanned` instead of 0, set `st.Scanned = st.Buffer.Length` after a fruitless scan, and reset it to 0 after a Remove/Clear. Alternatively buffer the chunk in the char[] and search the freshly-read span only. This drops the cost to O(N).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source and re-derived independently.

ALGORITHM (EngineClient.cs:588-633): The outer `while(true)` loop re-runs the inner `for (int i = 0; i < st.Buffer.Length; i++)` newline scan (lines 591-595) from index 0 on every iteration, then reads exactly one fixed 16 KiB chunk (line 619; `st.Chunk` is `new char[16*1024]`, line 72) and appends it (line 632). The buffer only shrinks via Remove/Clear AFTER a frame completes, so for a single newline-terminated frame of N chars arriving in K=N/16384 reads the total scan work is 16K+32K+...+N = N(K+1)/2 ≈ N²/(2·16384) StringBuilder indexer accesses — O(N²/chunkSize), exactly as claimed. There is no scan-offset memo; the suggested `Scanned` field does not exist. For a 32 MiB frame (~2048 chunks) that is ~34 billion index accesses; ~3.3 billion at 10 MiB. The accesses are `StringBuilder.this[int]`, which on .NET's chunked StringBuilder walks the internal chunk chain, so the constant factor is genuinely worse than a flat array (finder's note is accurate).

TRIGGER REACHABILITY: The engine emits the whole plan as ONE event — restructure.rs:263 `sink.send(...RestructurePlan(Wrap::new(plan)))` with all moves in a single Vec — and sink.rs:85-86 `serde_json::to_writer` (compact, no internal newlines) + `write_all(b"\n")`, i.e. one newline-delimited line. Each IpcMove carries file_id + two full Windows paths (source, destination) + category + tier + confidence + reason (restructure.rs:228-261), ~300-600 B serialized. A 50k–200k-file library yields a 10–60 MiB single line. The C# cap was deliberately raised to 32 MiB (lines 56-64) specifically to admit these large single-frame plans, so this is the designed happy path, not an error path.

THREAD / HARM (LOGIC lens): The finder did NOT misattribute the thread — this runs on `_stdoutLoop` (line 544 Task.Run), not the UI thread, and they say so. While the loop is CPU-bound for seconds, RestructurePlanEvent cannot be decoded/dispatched (lines 696/705 → arm 1186-1188), so LastRestructurePlan stays null and the Restructure tab cannot populate, and every event queued behind it on stdout is stalled too. No early-exit/memo/mitigation exists. Small corpora make the O(N²) term negligible, explaining why it passes small-corpus testing — matches the 'only the tens-of-thousands user is bitten' claim.

Minor caveat (why 0.9 not higher): it is a multi-second-to-minute CPU-bound stall of plan delivery on a background thread, not a UI-thread fast-fail or a hard deadlock; the precise wall-clock depends on hardware and could be a few seconds rather than 'minutes' for a 10 MiB plan. But it is genuinely present, triggerable on the documented target scenario, and user-visible (empty Restructure tab + a pegged core). The suggested incremental-offset fix correctly restores O(N).

Verified against the real source and proven empirically.

CODE (EngineClient.cs): ReadBoundedFrameAsync's inner loop (lines 592-595) scans `for (int i = 0; i < st.Buffer.Length; i++)` for '\n' over the ENTIRE StringBuilder from index 0 on every outer while(true) iteration, and only then reads a 16 KiB chunk (line 619, st.Chunk is char[16*1024] at line 72) and appends it (line 632). No incremental scan offset exists — StdoutFraming (lines 69-77) holds only Buffer/Chunk/Resyncing/OversizeDropped. So a single N-char frame built from N/16K reads costs sum-of-buffer-length = O(N^2/chunkSize) indexer accesses, and StringBuilder's chunked indexer is itself O(chunks), making it worse. MaxFrameChars = 32 MiB (line 64) was deliberately raised (comment 55-63) precisely so a full restructure plan fits in ONE frame rather than being dropped — guaranteeing a multi-MiB plan reaches this quadratic path.

ENGINE: RestructurePlan (ipc/mod.rs:887) serializes the whole moves Vec as one JSON object; sink.rs write_frame (81-88) emits it as one serde_json::to_writer + write_all(b\"\\n\") + flush = a single newline-delimited frame. RestructureMove (mod.rs:273-291) carries source+destination full paths, category, tier, confidence, and a free-text reason, so ~300 B/move is conservative. 14k moves ~= 4 MiB, 50k ~= 15 MiB — both under the 32 MiB cap and thus fully decoded through the scan.

EMPIRICAL (.NET 8.0.27, the app's exact target framework, simulating the precise framing loop): 1 MiB frame = 34M indexer accesses = 766 ms; 4 MiB frame = 539M accesses = 132 SECONDS; 8/16 MiB never finished within a 240 s timeout. Wall-clock grew ~172x for a 4x size increase (super-quadratic), confirming the StringBuilder-indexer compounding effect the claim cites.

THREAD-REALITY: The loop runs on a background Task.Run thread (line 544), so it does not freeze the UI thread directly — but the claim doesn't assert that. It correctly asserts the plan cannot be decoded (IpcCoder.Decode, line 696) or dispatched (_ui.TryEnqueue(Apply), line 705) until ReadBoundedFrameAsync returns, so the Restructure tab cannot populate for minutes while a core is pegged, on exactly the large-library scenario the product targets. Sub-MiB frames on small corpora pass unnoticed, explaining why it survived testing. Trigger, harm, and reachability all confirmed. The suggested fix (incremental Scanned offset) is the correct O(N) remedy. Confidence 0.97 (minor caveat: a true 200k-move/60 MiB plan would exceed the 32 MiB cap and instead hit the resync/drop path, but the 12k–110k-move range, i.e. 3.5–32 MiB, lands squarely in the quadratic-decode zone — the core bug stands).

VERIFIED REAL. Re-derived independently from source.

Mechanism (EngineClient.cs:586-633): ReadBoundedFrameAsync's outer loop (1) scans the ENTIRE buffer for '\n' from index 0 via `for (int i = 0; i < st.Buffer.Length; i++)` (lines 592-595), then (2) reads exactly ONE 16 KiB chunk (line 619, st.Chunk is char[16*1024] at line 72) and appends it (line 632). There is no cached scan offset. For a frame of N chars assembled from N/16K chunk reads, before the terminating newline the buffer never contains '\n', so iteration k rescans ~k·16K chars. Total ≈ Σ(k·16K) = N²/(2·16K) char accesses — the claimed O(N²/chunkSize).

The StringBuilder multiplier is also correct and the bug is WORSE than stated: st.Buffer is a StringBuilder (line 71), target is net8.0 (csproj line 15), whose char indexer walks the chunk linked-list (m_ChunkPrevious). A forward `sb[i]` scan is the textbook O(n·chunks) pitfall, so each scan is super-linear by itself.

Reachability on the HAPPY path is confirmed by design: the cap was deliberately raised to 32 MiB (line 64) with the comment (lines 55-64) stating it must hold a full restructure plan (~200k moves × ~300 B) in ONE frame; the 1 MiB cap previously DROPPED it. Engine side confirms single-line emission: ipc/sink.rs:85-86 (serde_json::to_writer + write_all(b"\n")), and RestructurePlan carries `moves: Vec<RestructureMove>` inline (ipc/mod.rs:889), so a 50k-move plan = one ~10-16 MiB line. So a 16 MiB frame ≈ 1024 chunk reads → ~8.6 billion comparisons even at the optimistic O(1)-indexer bound (multi-second), and tens of billions with the indexer chunk-walk (minutes) — matching the claim.

Harm under the IMPACT lens: not a fast-fail crash. StdoutLoopAsync runs on a Task.Run thread-pool thread (line 544, ConfigureAwait(false) line 660) and marshals to UI via _ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev)) (line 705), so the UI thread isn't directly blocked. BUT Apply(ev) only runs AFTER ReadBoundedFrameAsync returns the frame — so the Restructure tab cannot populate until the multi-second-to-minutes spin completes, pegging a CPU core. That is a real user-visible stall on precisely the large-library scenario the product targets, not benign/cosmetic.

Confidence 0.9 (not 1.0) only because exact wall-clock depends on JIT/CPU and the very largest plans (>32 MiB) instead hit the oversize-drop path (lines 612-617); the harmful slow path is the in-cap 10-32 MiB range, which is squarely the targeted use case. The suggested fix (incremental Scanned offset, or scanning the freshly-read Chunk span before append) is the correct O(N) remedy.

confirmed.title: Anchor-tier moves are mapped to the informational "Keep" outcome, then silently applied without any review UI (files the user is told stay put actually move)
confirmed.description:
OutcomeForTier maps the engine's per-move `tier` field: "Mixed" -> Tidy, "Anchor" -> Keep, everything else -> Reorganize. The engine stamps a move's `tier` from its SOURCE FOLDER's classification (commands/restructure.rs:233-243), and a folder is classified "Anchor" when >=80% of its proposed moves share one destination category (pipeline/restructure.rs:121-127). Critically, `classify()` (pipeline/restructure.rs:148) emits a ProposedMove for EVERY non-failed file (the plan query is `WHERE f.failed = 0` with no "already-in-place" filter, commands/restructure.rs:33-34), so Anchor folders genuinely contribute moves to plan.Moves — both the homogeneous majority being relocated/renamed and the <=20% outliers. All of those moves carry tier="Anchor".

On the Windows app side these become RestructureOutcome.Keep rows in RestructureView.SyncPlan (line 212): each lands in _allFileRows with the row's default IsSelected=true (RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38) and in _filesByOutcome[Keep]. But the Keep recommendation card is built informational=true (RestructureView.SyncPlan:230-235 / AddRec), which means: no inline Files list (AddRec only populates Files when !informational, line 310), no See-All, no approve/skip toggle (RestructureRecommendationVm.ActionsVisibility/CountVisibility = Collapsed), and the stat tile/headline say "Keep N folders untouched ... nothing about them changes" / "folders kept intact". The user is given NO affordance to see or deselect these rows.

Yet RecomputeSelection (RestructureView.xaml.cs:355-364) sums selected files across ALL buckets including Keep, and ApplyAsync (lines 620-624) applies every move in plan.Moves whose _allFileRows row IsSelected — which includes all default-selected Keep rows. Result: clicking "Apply as shortcuts"/"Apply moves" relocates the Anchor/"Keep" files even though the UI promised they stay put, with no way to review or exclude them.

This is a Windows-only divergence from the macOS reference. On macOS, outcomeFor(p) switches on p.kind which is only .dissolved (->reorganize) or .movedOutAsOutlier (->tidy) — macOS proposals NEVER map to .keep, the .keep card is purely informational (RestructureView.swift:391, isInformational:true) and is NOT backed by any proposal, and selectedIDs = Set(result.proposals.map(\.fileID)) therefore never contains keep files. The Windows port mis-maps the source-folder "Anchor" tier onto the informational Keep outcome and folds those moves into the applied set.
confirmed.trigger: Pick a library whose folders include at least one well-organized homogeneous folder (>=80% of its files share a destination category, e.g. a year/person/document folder) plus a few outliers. Open the Restructure tab and let it plan. The plan stamps those folder's moves tier="Anchor". The Keep card shows "N folders kept intact / nothing changes" with no file list and no toggle, but ApplyBarSelectedCount silently includes those files (all default-selected). Click Apply (shortcuts or moves): the engine relocates/renames the supposedly-kept files because their default-selected rows are in the applied set, contradicting the on-screen guarantee and with no way to opt out.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Exclude Keep-outcome moves from the actionable/applied set so the Windows behavior matches macOS (where keep is purely informational and never in selectedIDs). Minimal fix: in RestructureView.SyncPlan, do not register tier=Anchor moves into _allFileRows/_filesByOutcome[Keep] for selection purposes (only count their folders for the informational stat); and in RecomputeSelection/ApplyAsync, skip RestructureOutcome.Keep rows. Alternatively, treat outlier-from-Anchor moves as Tidy (matching macOS .movedOutAsOutlier) by deriving the outcome per-move from whether the file is the homogeneous majority vs an outlier rather than from the whole source folder's Anchor tier. Either way, guarantee that no file shown under "Keep / folders kept intact" can be applied without explicit review.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain end-to-end; the bug is genuine, triggerable, and harmful.

ENGINE SIDE (confirms Anchor folders contribute real moves carrying tier="Anchor"):
- pipeline/restructure.rs:148-205 `classify()` pushes a ProposedMove for EVERY file in `files` (loop body line 153-203 has no "already in place" filter; the destination is always the canonical category tree, e.g. library_root/Photos/<Year>/<Month>/<file>). The plan query (commands/restructure.rs:33-34) is `WHERE f.failed = 0` only, so every non-failed file becomes a move.
- pipeline/restructure.rs:84-137 `classify_folders()` groups those moves by source folder; line 121-127 classifies a folder Anchor when homogeneity >= 0.80 (>=80% of its moves share one destination category). All moves in that folder (homogeneous majority AND <=20% outliers) are then stamped tier="Anchor" by commands/restructure.rs:233-237 (`tier_by_folder.get(parent)`), because the tier is the SOURCE FOLDER's classification, not a per-file property.

APP SIDE (confirms Anchor->Keep moves are silently folded into the applied set with no review UI):
- RestructureGrouping.cs:11-16 maps tier "Anchor" -> RestructureOutcome.Keep.
- RestructureView.xaml.cs SyncPlan (210-221) iterates ALL plan.Moves, creating a RestructureFileRowVm for every move (incl. Anchor/Keep) into _allFileRows and _filesByOutcome[Keep]. Rows default IsSelected=true (RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38).
- The Keep card is built informational=true (SyncPlan 230-235) with fileCount:0; AddRec only populates the inline Files list when !informational (310-313), so the Keep card has zero file rows. RestructureRecommendationVm ActionsVisibility/CountVisibility = Collapsed when IsInformational (cs:37-38), and the XAML gates the Review/Approve buttons on ActionsVisibility (RestructureView.xaml:384) and the file list on ExpandedVisibility (line 429), which can only be opened by the (collapsed) Review button. Net: no expand, no See-All, no approve/skip, no per-file checkbox for Keep.
- Yet RecomputeSelection (350-364) sums selected files across ALL _filesByOutcome buckets including Keep, and ApplyAsync (620-624) applies every plan.Moves entry whose _allFileRows row IsSelected — so all default-selected Anchor rows are applied. The on-screen copy says "folders kept intact / nothing about them changes."

HARM IS REAL (not nullified by the apply layer): restructure_apply.rs:137-145 skips a move only when source==destination (a true no-op). An Anchor folder is classified purely on destination-category homogeneity, NOT on whether it already sits at the canonical path. A homogeneous folder like "Summer 2023" of 2023 photos is Anchor, but source=...\Summer 2023\IMG.jpg vs destination=...\Photos\2023\July\IMG.jpg are unequal, so the no-op skip does NOT fire and move_file relocates the file. The only case where Keep files don't move is when the folder is already AT the canonical destination — which is the rarer subset the UI copy actually describes. In the common trigger case the supposedly-kept files are physically moved/renamed. Symlink mode (use_symlinks) bypasses the no-op skip entirely (line 142 gated on !use_symlinks) and creates links for them too.

MACOS DIVERGENCE confirmed: RestructureView.swift Proposal.kind has only .dissolved/.movedOutAsOutlier (82-83); outcomeFor (944-948) can only return .reorganize/.tidy, never .keep; the .keep card is isInformational:true (397) backed by no proposal; selectedIDs=Set(result.proposals.map(\.fileID)) (779) and applySelected filters proposals by selectedIDs (811-812), so keep files are never applied. The Windows port mis-maps the source-folder Anchor tier onto the informational Keep outcome and folds those moves into the applied set.

Confidence 0.9 rather than 1.0 only because realizing the data-loss requires a real library where an Anchor folder is not already at its canonical destination (the overwhelmingly common case for an unorganized library, but a user whose Anchor folders happen to already be canonically placed would see those moves no-op-skipped). The UI/selection mis-mapping and the absence of any review affordance for Keep rows are unconditional and incontrovertible.

Independently re-derived the full data flow; every load-bearing claim checks out against the actual source.

ENGINE: PLAN_FILES_SQL is "FROM files f WHERE f.failed = 0" with no already-in-place filter (commands/restructure.rs:33-34); classify() (pipeline/restructure.rs:148-205) and the semantic path push a ProposedMove for EVERY file (no skip), so plan.Moves covers all files, not just files needing to move. classify_folders marks a folder Anchor when homogeneity >= 0.80 (pipeline/restructure.rs:121-127); each move's IPC `tier` is stamped from its SOURCE FOLDER (commands/restructure.rs:233-237), so both the homogeneous majority AND the <=20% outliers of an Anchor folder carry tier="Anchor".

APP: OutcomeForTier maps "Anchor" => RestructureOutcome.Keep (RestructureGrouping.cs:14). SyncPlan creates a RestructureFileRowVm (default IsSelected=true, RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38) for every move and registers it in _allFileRows + _filesByOutcome[Keep] (RestructureView.xaml.cs:210-221). The Keep card is built informational:true (line 232-235), so: no inline Files (AddRec only fills Files when !informational, line 310-313), MatchedCount=0 -> SeeAllVisibility Collapsed (RestructureRecommendationVm.cs:101,308), Actions/CountVisibility Collapsed (RestructureRecommendationVm.cs:37-38). The headline/stat say "Keep N folders untouched ... nothing about them changes" / "folders kept intact" (lines 233-234,253). There is no affordance to view or deselect Keep rows. Yet RecomputeSelection sums ALL buckets including Keep (lines 355-364) and ApplyAsync adds every plan.Move whose _allFileRows row IsSelected (lines 620-624) — i.e. all default-selected Keep files — then calls ApplyRestructureAsync. The engine executes them (restructure_apply.rs:65-215); the only escape is the exact source==dest no-op skip (line 142-145), which does NOT fire for an anchor folder with a non-canonical name nor for the outlier subset.

macOS REFERENCE (confirms Windows-only divergence): RestructureEngine.compute emits NO proposal for anchor-tier files (kind=nil; continue, RestructureView.swift:1164-1165,1178) and none for the mixed-folder majority (1170-1172); outcomeFor has only .dissolved/.movedOutAsOutlier cases, never .keep (944-948); selectedIDs = Set(result.proposals.map(\.fileID)) (779) so keep files are never selectable/applied.

Lens (trigger/thread): not a threading issue — a synchronous UI-thread data-flow + UI-contract violation, fully reachable. Triggerable whenever a library has an Anchor folder whose files aren't already at their canonical destination, plus any Anchor folder's outliers. Harm matches high/correctness/data-movement: files the UI guarantees stay put are moved/renamed with no opt-out.

Confidence 0.9 rather than higher only because (a) a subset of anchor files may coincidentally be source==dest no-ops that don't actually move, and (b) the truly destructive path is "Apply moves" (the prominently-narrated "Apply as shortcuts" leaves originals but still creates shortcuts, itself contradicting "nothing changes"). Neither caveat refutes the bug.

Independently verified the full chain. ENGINE: pipeline/restructure.rs:148-205 `classify()` emits a ProposedMove for EVERY file in the slice with NO source==destination filter and no "stays put" notion (destination is always a freshly computed library_root/<Category>/<Year>/.../filename); the plan query (commands/restructure.rs:33-34) is `WHERE f.failed = 0` only. A folder is Anchor at homogeneity >= 0.80 (restructure.rs:121-127), and every move is stamped tier from its source folder (commands/restructure.rs:208-237), so Anchor folders' moves all carry tier="Anchor". APP: RestructureGrouping.cs:14 maps "Anchor"=>Keep; SyncPlan (RestructureView.xaml.cs:210-221) registers every move including Keep into _allFileRows + _filesByOutcome[Keep] with RestructureFileRowVm default IsSelected=true (RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38); the Keep card is built informational:true (line 230-235) so it has no Files list (AddRec line 310), no See-All, no approve/skip toggle, and the headline/tile read "folders kept intact / nothing about them changes" — the user gets no affordance to see or deselect. Yet RecomputeSelection (355-364) sums selected files across ALL buckets including Keep, and ApplyAsync (620-624) sends every plan.Moves move whose row IsSelected — all default-selected Keep rows. macOS DIVERGENCE confirmed: RestructureView.swift:1164-1165 `.anchor: kind = nil // stays put, no proposal` + the guard at 1178 means anchor files are never proposals; outcomeFor (944-947) has no .keep case; selectedIDs = Set(result.proposals.map(\.fileID)) (779) therefore never contains keep files; the keep card (391-398) is isInformational:true backed by summary counts. Windows folds keep moves into the applied set; macOS cannot. IMPACT: real data movement, bounded by one mitigation I checked — the engine apply has a no-op guard (restructure_apply.rs:137-145) that skips a real move when paths_equal(source,dest), counting it applied but NOT moving it. That guard only fires for files already at the engine's EXACT canonical scheme (library_root/Photos/Year/Month/..., library_root/People/Name/Year/...). A typical Anchor folder is user-organized their own way (e.g. D:\Lib\Alice\ -> dest D:\Lib\People\Alice\2023\..., or D:\Lib\Vacation2023\ -> D:\Lib\Photos\2023\March\...), so source != dest, the guard does NOT fire, and "Apply moves" genuinely relocates/renames files the UI promised would stay put — with no review path. Confidence < 1.0 only because the harm magnitude depends on the user's existing layout: for the narrow subset of folders already in the engine's canonical tree the no-op guard neutralizes the move, but for the common Anchor case the relocation is real. Symlink mode lacks even the no-op guard. The suggested fix (exclude Keep-outcome rows from _allFileRows/_filesByOutcome for selection, and skip Keep in RecomputeSelection/ApplyAsync, matching macOS where keep is never in selectedIDs) is correct.

confirmed.title: ModelInstallerService.Reset() raises AllInstalled/IsBusy PropertyChanged off the UI thread during RestartAsync → RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD
confirmed.description: ModelSlot.Set was hardened (V15.2.1) to marshal PropertyChanged onto the captured UI dispatcher, but its sibling class ModelInstallerService still has a naked Set<T> (lines 949-954) that raises PropertyChanged synchronously on the calling thread. RecomputeAggregates() writes AllInstalled and IsBusy through that naked Set, and AcceleratorIsRealInstall does the same (line 104). EngineClient.RestartAsync() awaits StopAndWaitForExitAsync(...).ConfigureAwait(false) (EngineClient.Commands.cs:282), so the continuation that calls StartAsync() (line 289) runs on a thread-pool thread. StartAsync()'s synchronous prefix calls ModelInstallerService.Instance.Reset() (EngineClient.cs:412) BEFORE any await, so Reset() → SeedFromSentinels() → RecomputeAggregates() → Set(ref _allInstalled/_isBusy) all execute on that pool thread. WelcomeSheet.xaml binds IsEnabled="{x:Bind InstallAllEnabled(Svc.AllInstalled, Svc.IsBusy), Mode=OneWay}" and Content="{x:Bind SkipLabel(Svc.AllInstalled), Mode=OneWay}" (WelcomeSheet.xaml:422,429), so the off-thread PropertyChanged propagates a write into a Button's IsEnabled/Content DependencyProperty off the UI thread — a native RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD fast-fail (the exact V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4 crash class). This is the one Set in this area that was missed when ModelSlot.Set was fixed.
confirmed.trigger: First-launch (or any launch with a required model still missing) keeps the WelcomeSheet alive, so its x:Bind to Svc.AllInstalled/Svc.IsBusy is live. The user installs a GPU Performance Pack and clicks 'Restart engine' (SettingsView OnRestartEngineClicked/OnRestartEngineCardClicked → RestartAsync). RestartAsync's ConfigureAwait(false) resumes StartAsync on a pool thread; Reset()→RecomputeAggregates() raises AllInstalled/IsBusy PropertyChanged off-thread; x:Bind writes Button.IsEnabled off the UI thread and the process fast-fails with COMException 0x8001010E.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Give ModelInstallerService the same UI-dispatcher marshaling ModelSlot.Set already has: capture _ui at ctor time (already done, line 124) and in Set<T> do `if (_ui is null || _ui.HasThreadAccess) handler(...); else _ui.TryEnqueue(() => handler(...));` before invoking PropertyChanged. Alternatively, marshal the whole Reset()/SeedFromSentinels() body onto _ui. This closes AllInstalled, IsBusy, and AcceleratorIsRealInstall in one place.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full chain against source; the defect is genuinely present, off-thread reachable, and harmful.

1) The naked Set is real. ModelInstallerService.Set<T> (ModelInstallerService.cs:949-954) invokes PropertyChanged synchronously on the calling thread with NO UI marshaling. Its sibling ModelSlot.Set<T> (ModelSlot.cs:303-324) DOES marshal: `if (_ui is null || _ui.HasThreadAccess) handler(...); else _ui.TryEnqueue(() => handler(...))`. So the asymmetry the finding describes ("the one Set missed when ModelSlot was fixed") is exactly correct. ModelInstallerService captures _ui at ctor (line 128) but only uses it in the watchdog (line 662), never in Set.

2) AllInstalled (line 485), IsBusy (line 492), and AcceleratorIsRealInstall (line 104) all write through that naked Set. RecomputeAggregates (495-511) writes AllInstalled/IsBusy; SeedFromSentinels writes AcceleratorIsRealInstall=true at line 540.

3) Off-thread reachability confirmed: EngineClient.StartAsync (EngineClient.cs:381) calls ModelInstallerService.Instance.Reset() at line 412 as the FIRST statement of its try block, before `State = LifecycleState.Starting` (414) and before any await. RestartAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:279) does `await StopAndWaitForExitAsync(...).ConfigureAwait(false)` (282) then `await StartAsync().ConfigureAwait(false)` (289). StopAndWaitForExitAsync uses ConfigureAwait(false) on ShutdownAsync (250) and Task.Delay(100) (266); the delay essentially always runs at least once on restart (the process can't exit instantly after the Shutdown IPC), so the continuation that invokes StartAsync runs on a thread-pool thread. StartAsync's synchronous prefix — including Reset() -> SeedFromSentinels() -> RecomputeAggregates() and the AcceleratorIsRealInstall write — therefore executes on that pool thread.

4) Live x:Bind targets confirmed in WelcomeSheet.xaml: IsEnabled="{x:Bind InstallAllEnabled(Svc.AllInstalled, Svc.IsBusy), Mode=OneWay}" (422), Content="{x:Bind SkipLabel(Svc.AllInstalled), Mode=OneWay}" (429), plus FIVE bindings to Svc.AcceleratorIsRealInstall (344,345,359,390,395) driving FontIcon.Glyph/Foreground, TextBlock.Text, and Visibility. WinUI x:Bind OneWay does NOT auto-marshal INotifyPropertyChanged; it writes the target DependencyProperty synchronously on the raising thread, so an off-UI-thread PropertyChanged writes a DependencyProperty off-thread -> RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD (0x8001010E). This matches the exact bug class documented in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md (V15.2/V15.2.1 ModelSlot, V15.4 SidebarQueueList).

5) The described trigger is the most directly reachable: user installs the GPU Performance Pack, so the ort_cuda_x64 sentinel now exists on disk; they click Restart engine (SettingsView.OnRestartEngineClicked, line 1011, or the card handler line 1039 -> RestartAsync). On the pool thread, SeedFromSentinels sees the new sentinel and sets AcceleratorIsRealInstall = true (line 540). If the welcome sheet was seeded before the install completed (AcceleratorIsRealInstall == false), the naked Set's equality check (951) passes and PropertyChanged fires off-thread, writing the Accelerator FontIcon/TextBlock/Visibility off the UI thread -> fast-fail. Notably the crash does NOT even require AllInstalled/IsBusy to flip — AcceleratorIsRealInstall is the cleanest reachable change in the exact stated scenario.

Confidence is 0.85 rather than higher only because of one scenario-dependence I could not fully prove from code alone: whether the WelcomeSheet is still loaded (its x:Bind subscriptions live) at the moment the Settings restart fires. The finding's premise (first-launch with a required model missing keeps the sheet alive as an overlay while the user reaches Settings) is plausible and the sheet's subscriptions persist until Unloaded, but I did not verify the exact lifecycle/overlay layering that keeps it in the tree concurrently with the Settings tab. Apart from that, every load-bearing claim (naked Set, off-thread call chain via ConfigureAwait(false), Reset before any await, live x:Bind to all three properties) is confirmed in source. The suggested fix (mirror ModelSlot.Set's _ui.HasThreadAccess/TryEnqueue marshaling in ModelInstallerService.Set) is correct and closes AllInstalled, IsBusy, and AcceleratorIsRealInstall in one place.

Verified every link of the claimed chain against source and re-derived it independently; the threading reality holds.

1) RestartAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:279) awaits StopAndWaitForExitAsync(...).ConfigureAwait(false) (line 282) then `await StartAsync().ConfigureAwait(false)` (line 289). With ConfigureAwait(false) no SynchronizationContext is captured, so the continuation that CALLS StartAsync runs on a thread-pool thread. Confirmed.

2) StartAsync (EngineClient.cs:381) executes `ModelInstallerService.Instance.Reset()` at line 412 — synchronously, before any await in the method body. So Reset() runs on the pool thread. Confirmed.

3) Reset() (ModelInstallerService.cs:275) ends in SeedFromSentinels() (line 294); SeedFromSentinels (line 525) ends in RecomputeAggregates() (line 542) — both synchronous, still on the pool thread. RecomputeAggregates (line 495) writes AllInstalled (setter line 485) and IsBusy (setter line 492) through ModelInstallerService.Set<T>.

4) ModelInstallerService.Set<T> (lines 949-954) raises `PropertyChanged?.Invoke(this, ...)` synchronously on the calling thread with NO UI marshaling — despite _ui being captured at ctor (lines 124/128). Its hardened sibling ModelSlot.Set (ModelSlot.cs:303-324) does exactly the missing guard (`if (_ui is null || _ui.HasThreadAccess) handler(...); else _ui.TryEnqueue(...)`, lines 316-322). This is the documented V15.2.1 fix that was applied to ModelSlot but NOT to ModelInstallerService — the asymmetry the claim alleges is real.

5) WelcomeSheet subscribes Svc.PropertyChanged (WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:51; Svc => ModelInstallerService.Instance, line 34) and binds IsEnabled="{x:Bind InstallAllEnabled(Svc.AllInstalled, Svc.IsBusy), Mode=OneWay}" (WelcomeSheet.xaml:422) and Content="{x:Bind SkipLabel(Svc.AllInstalled), Mode=OneWay}" (line 429). A PropertyChanged for AllInstalled/IsBusy makes x:Bind write Button.IsEnabled/Button.Content (DependencyProperties) off the UI thread → RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD / COMException 0x8001010E. This is the exact crash class platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md flags (V15.2 ThumbnailService, V15.2.1 ModelSlot, V15.4 SidebarQueueList).

6) Trigger is reachable: OnRestartEngineClicked/OnRestartEngineCardClicked (SettingsView.xaml.cs:1005/1032) call RestartAsync from the UI thread; WelcomeSheet stays alive while a required model is missing, keeping the x:Bind live.

One honest caveat (why not higher confidence): Set<T> short-circuits when EqualityComparer.Default.Equals(field, value) is true (line 951), so the off-thread PropertyChanged fires only when AllInstalled/IsBusy actually TRANSITIONS during the off-thread Reset. Reachable transition states clearly exist — FailIfDownloading flipping a Downloading gating slot (e.g. RamPlus) to Failed drops IsBusy true→false, or SeedFromSentinels picking up a newly-finished model flips AllInstalled false→true — but whether any given restart hits one is transient-state-dependent, so it is not a guaranteed-every-time crash. The structural defect (a naked non-marshaling Set reachable on a pool thread that x:Bind forwards into a DependencyProperty) is unambiguous and matches three prior fast-fail bugs of identical shape; the codebase's own convention treats this as a crash-in-waiting, not a benign rare path. The suggested fix (mirror ModelSlot.Set's _ui.TryEnqueue guard in ModelInstallerService.Set, closing AllInstalled, IsBusy, and AcceleratorIsRealInstall at line 104) is correct.

The underlying code asymmetry is REAL but the claimed crash is NOT reachable via the described trigger; the two required conditions are mutually exclusive.

CONFIRMED mechanics:
- ModelInstallerService.Set<T> (ModelInstallerService.cs:949-954) raises PropertyChanged synchronously on the calling thread with NO dispatcher marshaling, unlike the hardened sibling ModelSlot.Set<T> (ModelSlot.cs:303-324, which does `_ui.TryEnqueue` when off-thread). RecomputeAggregates writes AllInstalled/IsBusy through this naked Set (lines 485,492,501-510) and AcceleratorIsRealInstall too (line 104).
- The off-thread path is genuine: RestartAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:282) awaits StopAndWaitForExitAsync(...).ConfigureAwait(false), which truly suspends (Task.Delay/ShutdownAsync), so the continuation calling StartAsync() (line 289) runs on a thread-pool thread. StartAsync()'s synchronous prefix calls ModelInstallerService.Instance.Reset() (EngineClient.cs:412) before any await, so Reset()->SeedFromSentinels()->RecomputeAggregates() runs on the pool thread. The existing app-audit (audit-2026-06-04b/app-audit.json finding #2) independently verified this same off-thread continuation. x:Bind OneWay writing DependencyProperties synchronously on the firing thread is the project's confirmed crash mechanism (verified in the People/Cleanup sibling findings via generated .g.cs).

DECISIVE REFUTATION — reachability:
1. The ONLY live x:Bind targets for Svc.AllInstalled/Svc.IsBusy in the entire app are WelcomeSheet.xaml:422 (Button.IsEnabled via InstallAllEnabled) and 429 (Button.Content via SkipLabel). SettingsView.xaml binds NEITHER (grep: no matches). So the off-thread PropertyChanged only has a DependencyProperty to corrupt while the WelcomeSheet is alive.
2. RestartAsync has exactly two callers, both SettingsView 'Restart engine' buttons (SettingsView.xaml.cs:1011, 1039) — the only off-thread Reset() source.
3. The WelcomeSheet is hosted in WelcomeOverlay (MainWindow.xaml:122-136): a Grid in Grid.Row='1' with a hit-testable opaque-ish background (#CC0D0D14), declared after and covering the ENTIRE content row, which contains both the sidebar nav (SidebarHost, Row 1 Col 0) and DetailHost/Settings (Row 1 Col 2). While the sheet is visible the user cannot reach the sidebar nav or SettingsView, so the 'Restart engine' buttons are unreachable. The stated trigger ('user clicks Restart engine in Settings while WelcomeSheet stays alive') is self-contradictory.
4. The OTHER callers of Reset()/StartAsync() (crash-respawn at EngineClient.cs:777 and the respawn StartAsync at line 820) run INSIDE `_ui.TryEnqueue(...)`, so their synchronous Reset() prefix executes ON the UI thread — safe. ConfigureAwait(false) inside those lambdas only affects post-await work, not the synchronous Reset().

Thus the off-thread Reset() (Settings/RestartAsync) and the live binding (WelcomeSheet) cannot coexist: when one is reachable the other is torn down/occluded. The naked Set is a legitimate defense-in-depth/consistency defect worth fixing to match ModelSlot, but it is not triggerable into the claimed RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD native fast-fail by the described scenario (or any other path I could find). Verdict: refuted (real latent inconsistency, not a triggerable harmful bug as claimed). Confidence held below max only because WinUI overlay hit-testing is a runtime behavior I verified by static reading of the XAML layout rather than on-hardware, the codebase's standing caveat.

confirmed.title: Anchor-tier ("Keep") moves are applied despite the UI promising those folders stay untouched
confirmed.description: The engine stamps tier="Anchor" on every move out of a source folder where ≥80% of its files go to one category (commands/restructure.rs lines 211-244; classify_folders only classifies folders that DO have moves). On the app side OutcomeForTier("Anchor") maps to RestructureOutcome.Keep (RestructureGrouping.cs:14). SyncPlan (lines 210-221) still builds a RestructureFileRowVm for these Anchor moves with IsSelected=true (default) and stores them in _filesByOutcome[Keep]. The Keep recommendation card, however, is created with informational:true and fileCount:0 (lines 230-236), and AddRec only populates vm.Files when !informational (line 310), so these rows are NEVER shown, have NO checkbox, and have NO 'See all' drill-down (SeeAllVisibility is collapsed because MatchedCount=0). The card text literally says 'Keep N folders untouched — nothing about them changes.' Yet ApplyAsync (lines 620-624) iterates plan.Moves and includes any move whose row.IsSelected is true, with no tier/outcome filter. Result: Anchor-tier files are physically moved (or symlinked) on Apply, directly contradicting the 'Keep ... untouched' promise, and the user has no UI affordance to exclude them. RecomputeSelection (lines 355-364) also counts them, so the 'X of Y selected' total is inflated by invisible rows.
confirmed.trigger: Plan a library that contains a homogeneous folder whose files move to one category (e.g. a 'Vacation2023' folder where ≥80% of photos route to Photos/2023). The engine classifies it Anchor; the Keep card shows it as 'staying put'; pressing 'Apply as shortcuts' or 'Convert to real moves' moves/symlinks those files anyway, with no way to deselect them.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Exclude Keep/informational outcomes from the applied move set and from the selected/total counts. In SyncPlan, do not create file rows for outcome==Keep (or mark them non-selectable), and in ApplyAsync filter to moves whose outcome is Tidy/Reorganize: `if (RestructureGrouping.OutcomeForTier(m.Tier) == RestructureOutcome.Keep) continue;` before the IsSelected check. Mirror the same filter in RecomputeSelection so the count matches what Apply sends.

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently re-derived the full call chain; the defect is genuinely present as written.

ENGINE SIDE (the premise the claim depends on): restructure.rs:84 `classify_folders` groups ONLY folders that appear in `moves` (lines 89-92) and tags a folder `Anchor` when its move-homogeneity >= 0.80 (lines 121-127). The doc comment at lines 80-83 is explicit: folders with NO moves are excluded ("implicit anchor folders intact" tier). So an Anchor folder by construction HAS moves, and those moves are stamped tier="Anchor" (restructure.rs:233-243). The claim's premise is correct — Anchor is not "files staying put"; it's "a homogeneous folder whose files DO move to one category." (The engine apply side also applies whatever the app sends, with no tier filter.)

APP SIDE:
- RestructureGrouping.cs:14 maps tier "Anchor" -> RestructureOutcome.Keep. Confirmed.
- SyncPlan (RestructureView.xaml.cs:210-221) builds a RestructureFileRowVm for EVERY move regardless of outcome and stores it in both _allFileRows[m.FileId] (line 214) and _filesByOutcome[Keep]. RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38 defaults IsSelected=true. Confirmed.
- The Keep recommendation card is created informational:true, fileCount:0 (lines 230-236). AddRec populates vm.Files only when !informational (line 310) and sets MatchedCount = informational ? 0 : ... (line 308). With Files empty and MatchedCount 0: SeeAllVisibility = (0 > 0) = Collapsed (RecommendationVm.cs:101), ActionsVisibility/CountVisibility Collapsed (lines 37-38), ExpandedVisibility list empty. So Keep rows have NO checkbox (the checkbox lives only inside the expand-in-place Files list, XAML lines 430-468), NO drill-down (SetOutcomeFilter for Keep is reachable only via the collapsed See-all button). The user has zero affordance to deselect them. Confirmed.
- ApplyAsync (lines 620-624) iterates plan.Moves and adds every move whose _allFileRows row.IsSelected is true, with NO tier/outcome filter. Anchor rows have IsSelected=true, so they ARE included in `sel` and sent to ApplyRestructureAsync (line 631). Confirmed — files are physically moved/symlinked.
- The card text (line 234) "nothing about them changes" plus "folders kept intact" (StayingPutSubtitle / KeepHint) directly contradicts that those files move. Confirmed harmful/data-loss-adjacent.

One correction to the finding's wording: RecomputeSelection's total (line 353) is plan.Moves.Count, which ALSO includes the Anchor moves, and `selected` sums the same _allFileRows the Apply uses — so the displayed "X of Y" is internally self-consistent with what Apply sends (it is NOT inflated relative to the actual apply set). The finder's claim that the count is "inflated by invisible rows" is the one inaccurate sub-point. But this does not save the bug: the count is still inflated relative to what the UI shows/promises, and the core defect (Keep/Anchor files moved despite the "untouched" promise, with no deselect affordance) is fully real and triggerable exactly as described. Confidence 0.9 (slightly under 1.0 only because confirming the exact >=80%-routes-out trigger fires in practice depends on real corpus data, but the code path is unconditional).

Verified end-to-end against source; the bug is a genuine Windows-vs-macOS divergence, triggerable and harmful.

ENGINE EMITS ANCHOR MOVES (not no-ops):
- pipeline/restructure.rs classify_folders (lines 84-137) groups only folders that HAVE moves; a non-generic folder with >2 files and homogeneity>=0.80 is FolderClassification::Anchor (line 123).
- commands/restructure.rs (lines 202-248) builds tier_by_folder and stamps tier="Anchor" on EVERY move whose parent is an anchor folder. It never drops anchor moves — proposed (from classify/semantic_classify) flows straight into plan.moves with only the tier added. Each move carries a real destination computed by classify() (e.g. <root>/Photos/2023/March/x.jpg) that differs from its source (e.g. <root>/Vacation2023/x.jpg).
- restructure_apply.rs only skips when source==destination (line 142); for an anchor folder with a non-category name the paths differ, so move_file/make_symlink (lines 161-165) physically relocates/symlinks the file.

APP INCLUDES THEM IN APPLY WITH NO AFFORDANCE TO EXCLUDE:
- RestructureGrouping.OutcomeForTier("Anchor") => Keep (RestructureGrouping.cs:14), confirmed by unit test and enum.
- SyncPlan (RestructureView.xaml.cs:210-221) creates a RestructureFileRowVm for EVERY move (incl. Anchor) and stores it in _allFileRows[m.FileId] AND _filesByOutcome[Keep]. RestructureFileRowVm._isSelected defaults to true (RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38).
- The Keep card is built informational:true, fileCount:0 (lines 230-236); AddRec only populates vm.Files when !informational (lines 310-313) and sets MatchedCount=0 (line 308). SeeAllVisibility = MatchedCount>Files.Count => 0>0 => Collapsed (VM line 101). ActionsVisibility/CountVisibility collapsed for informational (VM lines 37-38). XAML confirms the per-file CheckBox (lines 445-450) and "See all" button (lines 469-478) live inside the Files ItemsRepeater (line 430), which is empty for Keep — so there is literally no checkbox, no drill-down. StayingPutCard is a static "N folders kept intact" disclosure with no file list.
- ApplyAsync (lines 620-624) iterates plan.Moves and adds any move whose _allFileRows[m.FileId].IsSelected is true — NO tier/outcome filter — then ApplyRestructureAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:632-633) forwards them verbatim to the engine. So selected Anchor moves are applied and physically moved.
- RecomputeSelection (lines 350-364) iterates _filesByOutcome including the Keep bucket, inflating the "X of Y selected" total with invisible rows; total=plan.Moves.Count also includes them.

The UI promise "Keep N folders untouched — nothing about them changes" / "folders kept intact" therefore directly contradicts runtime behavior.

DIVERGENCE vs the macOS reference (the spec): RestructureView.swift compute() emits NO proposal for anchor-folder files (line 1147-1150 just counts staysPut; line 1164-1165 case .anchor: kind=nil // stays put, no proposal; line 1178 guard ... else continue). macOS's apply set structurally cannot contain anchor files, so the promise holds there. Windows lost this exclusion.

LENS (trigger-reality): This is pure deterministic data flow, not a thread/lifecycle question — fully reachable on the UI thread via the normal Plan→Apply sequence. The trigger (a meaningfully-named, homogeneous, non-category-shaped folder like Vacation2023) yields source!=destination, so the no-op guard does not absorb the moves. Confidence held just below certainty only because the harm requires the anchor folder's files not already to sit at their heuristic destination (else restructure_apply.rs:142 no-ops them) — but that is precisely the documented common case and the stated trigger. The suggested fix (filter Keep/informational moves out of ApplyAsync and RecomputeSelection, mirroring macOS) is correct.

Verified the full chain across engine + app + the macOS reference.

ENGINE: pipeline/restructure.rs::classify (lines 148-205) emits a ProposedMove for EVERY file with a heuristic destination (e.g. Photos/2023/March/foo.jpg) — no tier gating. classify_folders (lines 84-137) marks a homogeneous source folder Anchor (homogeneity>=0.80, total>2, non-generic name). commands/restructure.rs (lines 202-247) stamps tier="Anchor" onto those moves and KEEPS them in plan.moves. For a typical Anchor file the destination differs from the source folder, so it is a genuine move.

APP: RestructureGrouping.OutcomeForTier("Anchor") => Keep (RestructureGrouping.cs:14). SyncPlan (RestructureView.xaml.cs:210-221) builds a RestructureFileRowVm for every move including Anchor, with IsSelected defaulting to true (RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38), stored in _allFileRows + _filesByOutcome[Keep]. The Keep card is created informational:true/fileCount:0 (lines 230-236); AddRec only populates vm.Files when !informational (line 310) and sets MatchedCount=0 for informational (line 308), so the rows are never shown, have no checkbox, and no See-all drill-down. Card body literally says "...nothing about them changes." (line 234). ApplyAsync (lines 620-624) iterates plan.Moves and includes any move whose row.IsSelected is true with NO tier/outcome filter — so default-selected Anchor moves are sent to the engine. RecomputeSelection (lines 355-364) counts all _filesByOutcome including Keep, inflating the selected total.

ENGINE APPLY: restructure_apply.rs only no-ops when source==planned destination (lines 142-145); for an Anchor file source != destination, so MoveFileExW / CreateSymbolicLinkW actually runs. The containment/reparse guards do not exclude Anchor moves.

DIVERGENCE FROM THE BEHAVIORAL REFERENCE: macOS RestructureView.swift (lines 1164-1165) explicitly emits NO proposal for Anchor-folder files (kind = nil // stays put, no proposal; comment line 1094 "Files inside Anchor folders stay put — no proposals"). The Windows port includes them. This is a real port regression, not intended behavior.

IMPACT LENS: Not a crash/hang/leak. It is a correctness / data-movement bug — files the UI promises stay untouched are physically moved or symlinked on Apply, with no UI affordance to deselect them, and the count is inflated by invisible rows. Moves are reversible (DB update + recovery sidecar; symlink mode non-destructive), so it is not unrecoverable data loss, but the false "untouched" promise plus actual relocation is genuine, triggerable user-facing harm exactly as described. The suggested fix (filter Keep/informational out of the applied set and the counts, in ApplyAsync + RecomputeSelection, and/or skip building selectable rows for Keep in SyncPlan) is correct. Confidence 0.9 (slightly under 1.0 only because exact card wording differs trivially from the quoted text and real-data trigger frequency depends on having a homogeneous >2-file non-generic source folder, but the code path is unconditional once such a folder exists).

Key files: platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Restructure/RestructureView.xaml.cs (SyncPlan 200-295, AddRec 297-316, RecomputeSelection 350-380, ApplyAsync 616-644); platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/RestructureGrouping.cs:11-16; platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/RestructureFileRowVm.cs:38; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/restructure.rs:84-205; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/restructure.rs:202-264; platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/restructure_apply.rs:142-145; platforms/apple/app/Sources/FileID/Views/RestructureView.swift:1146-1178.

confirmed.title: Un-coordinated refresh entry points clobber each other's result set (no superseding guard)
confirmed.description: There are four independent producers of the grid contents — RefreshAsync (from Loaded, the throttled per-batch RequestLibraryRefresh, and OnUndoLastClicked, all passing CancellationToken.None), the debounced search RefreshAsync (via ScheduleRefresh's own _searchCts), FindSimilarAsync, and SemanticSearchWithSeedAsync. Only the search-vs-search case is coordinated: ScheduleRefresh's Interlocked.Exchange cancels the prior _searchCts so a stale search returns early at the `token.IsCancellationRequested` check. The other entry points each create their own linked token from _disposalCts only and have NO shared generation/superseding guard, so none of them cancels or is cancelled by the others. Each ends by calling ApplyOnUi(filtered) → ReplaceItems on the UI thread, ordered solely by completion time — last writer wins. Concrete trigger: user right-clicks a tile → 'Find similar' (FindSimilarAsync, token=None) during an active scan; a scan batch lands and OnEngineChanged fires RequestLibraryRefresh(force:false) → RefreshAsync(None). The full clip_embeddings scan in FindSimilarAsync is slow, so the batch RecentAsync refresh completes second and its ApplyOnUi overwrites the just-shown similar-files grid with the plain recent-files list — the user's explicit action is silently discarded with no error. The reverse (a slow FindSimilarAsync landing after a newer batch refresh) is equally possible. Collection integrity is fine (all mutations marshal to the UI thread); the defect is wrong displayed state, not a crash.
confirmed.trigger: Invoke 'Find similar' on a tile (or a Restructure similar-files lookup) while a scan is running so a per-batch RequestLibraryRefresh fires concurrently; whichever RefreshAsync/FindSimilarAsync completes last wins, so the similar-files grid gets replaced by recent files (or vice versa).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a single monotonically-increasing refresh-generation field (int, bumped via Interlocked.Increment at the top of every public refresh entry point) and capture it locally before the await; in ApplyOnUi (or just before ReplaceItems on the UI thread) bail when the captured generation != the current generation. This makes the newest user/engine-initiated refresh authoritative regardless of which producer's IO finishes last, mirroring the existing _searchCts superseding logic but unified across all entry points.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against C:/Users/adamm/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/LibraryViewModel.cs and the calling view LibraryView.xaml.cs. The defect is genuinely present.

CONFIRMED PRODUCERS, all converging on ApplyOnUi(filtered) -> ReplaceItems with NO superseding guard:
- RefreshAsync(CancellationToken.None): called from Loaded (LibraryView.xaml.cs:87), RequestLibraryRefresh (line 216, itself invoked by OnEngineChanged on LastBatch at line 131), and OnUndoLastClicked (line 268). Inside (LibraryViewModel.cs:274) the linked token is CreateLinkedTokenSource(ct, _disposalCts.Token); with ct=None the only cancellation source is _disposalCts.
- FindSimilarAsync(fileId, None): from OnContextFindSimilar (LibraryView.xaml.cs:1313). Linked only to _disposalCts (line 379).
- SemanticSearchWithSeedAsync: linked only to _disposalCts (line 340) — currently has no live caller, so it is a latent fourth producer, not active; this does not affect the cited trigger.
- The debounced search RefreshAsync via ScheduleRefresh (lines 549-571) is the ONLY coordinated path: Interlocked.Exchange(ref _searchCts, ...) cancels the prior CTS, so a superseded search returns at the token.IsCancellationRequested check (line 299). Nothing else participates in this.

LAST-WRITER-WINS confirmed: RefreshAsync (line 292) and FindSimilarAsync (line 391) both run their DB work via Task.Run(...).ConfigureAwait(false). Their cancellation checks (lines 299, 392) only test _disposed and their OWN token — neither cancels the other. There is no re-entrancy guard; IsLoading does not gate the grid write. ReplaceItems (lines 434-456) blindly applies whatever set it is handed via an Id-merge or Reset fast-path, so a 'recent files' result faithfully overwrites a just-shown 'similar files' set.

TRIGGER REACHABLE: During an active scan, EngineClient.LastBatch fires per batch (throttled to >=1s, LibraryView.xaml.cs:129/32). The View-level throttle (_lastReloadAt) is unrelated to the VM and does not coordinate with FindSimilarAsync. RequestLibraryRefresh does DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue(async () => await RefreshAsync(...)); the dispatcher is released at the first await (Task.Run), so the batch RefreshAsync and the user's FindSimilarAsync run concurrently. FindSimilarAsync's SimilarFilesAsync is documented as an unbounded clip_embeddings scan with no LIMIT (lines 386-390), so it can finish second and have its result clobbered by the faster paged RecentAsync — or the reverse. Either way the user's explicit action is silently discarded.

LOGIC-LENS CHECK (the finder did NOT misread): the writes ARE marshaled to the UI thread (ApplyOnUi, lines 410-420), so this is wrong-displayed-state, not a crash or collection corruption — exactly as the finding states. _disposalCts cleanup is irrelevant to this race (it only addresses tab-swap teardown). No existing mechanism cancels or supersedes across these producers.

Severity medium and the suggested fix (a monotonic generation counter bumped at each entry point and checked just before ReplaceItems, mirroring _searchCts) are both appropriate. Confidence held at 0.88 rather than higher because reproduction depends on a specific timing window during a scan and because SemanticSearchWithSeedAsync is currently dead code, slightly narrowing the active surface to the FindSimilar-vs-batch-RefreshAsync pair the trigger names.

Verified against LibraryViewModel.cs and LibraryView.xaml.cs. The claim holds under the trigger/thread-reality lens.

FOUR PRODUCERS CONFIRMED, all ending in ApplyOnUi(filtered)->ReplaceItems:
- RefreshAsync(CancellationToken.None) from Loaded (LibraryView.xaml.cs:87), per-batch RequestLibraryRefresh (xaml.cs:216), and OnUndoLastClicked (xaml.cs:268).
- Debounced search RefreshAsync via ScheduleRefresh with its own _searchCts (LibraryViewModel.cs:549-571).
- FindSimilarAsync(fileId, None) from the context-menu async-void handler OnContextFindSimilar (xaml.cs:1313).
- SemanticSearchWithSeedAsync(seed, None) (LibraryViewModel.cs:335) — defined as an entry point (no live caller found in src, but same shape).

COORDINATION GAP CONFIRMED: I grepped _searchCts — it is referenced ONLY in ScheduleRefresh (Interlocked.Exchange + Cancel of prior, lines 555-561) and Dispose (79-81). FindSimilarAsync and SemanticSearchWithSeedAsync do NOT touch _searchCts; each builds a linked token from ONLY ct (which is None for these paths) + _disposalCts.Token (lines 274, 340, 379). _disposalCts fires only in Dispose (line 78), so it never cancels a sibling refresh. There is NO generation/superseding guard anywhere — no Interlocked.Increment counter exists, and ApplyOnUi (410-420)/ReplaceItems (434-456)/MergeById (477-532) unconditionally apply whatever `next` they receive. No re-entrancy guard either: producers only check _disposed (337, 376), so they freely overlap.

RACE IS GENUINELY OFF-UI-THREAD-ORDERED: both RefreshAsync (line 292) and FindSimilarAsync (line 391) await Task.Run(...).ConfigureAwait(false), so the post-IO continuation that calls ApplyOnUi runs on a thread-pool thread; ApplyOnUi then enqueues ReplaceItems via _ui.TryEnqueue (418). The two ReplaceItems run on the UI dispatcher in completion order — last writer wins. Collection integrity is fine (all mutations marshal to UI thread), so the defect is wrong displayed state, not a crash, exactly as claimed.

TRIGGER IS REALISTIC: during a scan, EngineClient.LastBatch fires RequestLibraryRefresh(force:false) at ~1 Hz (LibraryReloadThrottle = 1s, xaml.cs:32). confirmed.title: IsLoading spinner cleared while another refresh is still in flight (concurrent refresh finally races)
confirmed.description: IsLoading is a simple bool toggled by every refresh method: set true in the prologue, set false in the finally. Concrete trigger: type a query so the 200 ms debounce refresh starts, then a scan batch lands and RequestLibraryRefresh fires a second RefreshAsync; the faster one's finally runs IsLoading=false while the slower one is still mid-scan, so the spinner blinks off and back on (or off and never re-shown if the second one's prologue already ran). Alternatively, once the generation guard from the related finding exists, only the current-generation refresh should own IsLoading and a superseded refresh must not touch it.

confirmed.title: Per-request CancellationToken is ignored after the request is dequeued (full shell+disk+decode work runs for scrolled-away tiles)
confirmed.description: RequestAsync(path, modifiedAt, ct) captures the caller's CancellationToken into ThumbnailRequest.Cancellation. DrainAsync checks req.Cancellation.IsCancellationRequested exactly once, BEFORE work begins (line 163). The actual render call passes the SERVICE-lifetime token instead: `await RenderAsync(req.Path, req.ModifiedAt, _uiDispatcher, ct)` where `ct` is DrainAsync's parameter (_cts.Token), not req.Cancellation. As a result, once a request is dequeued, a per-tile cancellation is never observed: the expensive shell IThumbnailProvider call (file.GetThumbnailAsync), the L2 disk read (ThumbnailDiskCache.TryReadAsync, which is also passed `ct`/_cts.Token via the dispatcher arg, not req.Cancellation), and the SetSourceAsync decode all run to completion. On a fast scroll over a 256-px tile grid (the channel is sized for 50+ req/s), tiles that the user has already scrolled past keep consuming shell/disk/decode work and produce now-useless BitmapImages that are still inserted into L1. This is wasted CPU/IO on the exact hot path the cache exists to protect, and on the 4 GB target box it inflates the working set with off-screen decodes.
confirmed.trigger: Scroll the Library grid quickly so a tile is requested, dequeued by the worker, and then recycled (LibraryViewModel cancels its per-tile token on cell recycle) while RenderAsync is mid-flight. The shell thumbnail extraction, disk read, and decode all still complete and the result is cached, rather than aborting at the cancellation.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Honor the per-request token: link it with the service token (e.g. `using var linked = CancellationTokenSource.CreateLinkedTokenSource(ct, req.Cancellation);`) and pass `linked.Token` into RenderAsync (and through TryReadAsync). Keep the existing OperationCanceledException filter so a per-request cancel completes the TCS null without faulting the worker (re-check: the current catch `when (ex is not OperationCanceledException)` would let a req-cancel OCE escape and kill DrainAsync, so also catch OCE explicitly and TrySetResult(null)/continue rather than letting it bubble out of the foreach).

confirmed.title: LibraryView never disposes its ReadStore — SQLite connection + SemaphoreSlim leak per tab navigation
confirmed.description: In the LibraryView constructor a ReadStore is created as a *local* variable (`var store = new ReadStore(paths)`, line 60). It is handed to `ClipSearchService` (which stores it in `_store` but explicitly does NOT own it — `ClipSearchService.Dispose()` only detaches the engine PropertyChanged subscription and drains `_inflight`, it never calls `_store.Dispose()`) and to `LibraryViewModel` (whose `Dispose()` comment says "ClipSearchService is owned by the view, disposed there" and which never disposes the store either). `LibraryView.OnUnloaded` (lines 296-324) carefully disposes `ViewModel`, `_clip`, `_thumbnails`, and the thumb CTSes, but there is no field holding `store` and no call to `store.Dispose()`/`DisposeAsync()` anywhere. ReadStore implements `IDisposable`/`IAsyncDisposable` precisely so its open ReadOnly+Shared-cache `SqliteConnection` and its `SemaphoreSlim _gate` get released deterministically (Dispose body at ReadStore.cs:667-672). Concrete trigger: open the Library tab (ctor opens a shared-cache read-only connection to the WAL DB via the Loaded handler's `store.OpenAsync`), navigate away (Unloaded fires, store not disposed), navigate back (a brand-new ReadStore + new connection is opened). Each round-trip leaks one open SQLite connection and one SemaphoreSlim; the connection is only reclaimed later by GC/finalization (SqliteConnection has a finalizer that closes the native handle) rather than deterministically, and only once the whole view graph becomes collectible. Over a session of repeated tab switches this accumulates open DB connections against the engine's WAL.
confirmed.trigger: Open Library tab, switch to another tab, switch back — repeat. Each cycle opens a new ReadOnly shared-cache SqliteConnection that is never Dispose()d; it survives until GC finalizes the orphaned view graph.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Keep the store in a view field (e.g. `private readonly ReadStore _store;`) and dispose it in OnUnloaded as the final teardown step, after ViewModel/_clip (so no in-flight read touches a disposed connection): `try { _store.Dispose(); } catch { }`. Alternatively make ClipSearchService own and dispose the ReadStore it was constructed with. Update the LibraryViewModel.Dispose comment accordingly.
