Thread/runtime-reality lens: OnUnloaded runs deterministically on tab swap on the UI thread; the bug is simply the absence of a disposal call, not a threading mirage. Each navigate-away/back round-trip leaks one open read-only WAL SqliteConnection and one SemaphoreSlim, reclaimed only by GC finalization of the orphaned view graph. Real, triggerable, and harmful (non-deterministic accumulation of open DB connections against the WAL); medium severity is apt. Confidence 0.95 — only reservation is that GC eventually reclaims them and a typical session has bounded tab switches, but the leak-per-cycle and missing-cleanup-path are incontrovertible.

Verified by reading the actual sources. The leak is real and triggerable, with the claimed (deterministic-disposal) harm.

EVIDENCE:
1. LibraryView ctor (LibraryView.xaml.cs:60) creates `var store = new ReadStore(paths)` as a LOCAL — never stored in a field. The only other references in the file (grep) are line 86 `store.OpenAsync` (captured by the Loaded lambda closure) and doc comments. No field, no `store.Dispose()` anywhere.
2. ReadStore (ReadStore.cs) genuinely holds two disposable resources: an open `SqliteConnection` (field line 33, opened ReadOnly + Cache=Shared, assigned at line 135) and a `SemaphoreSlim _gate` (line 34). Its `Dispose()` (lines 667-672) is the ONLY cleanup — it disposes `_connection` and `_gate`. There is no finalizer on ReadStore, so the native SQLite handle is reclaimed only by SqliteConnection's own finalizer.
3. ClipSearchService.Dispose() (lines 75-86) only detaches the EngineClient subscription and drains `_inflight`; it never touches `_store` (stored at line 71). Confirmed it does NOT own the store.
4. LibraryViewModel.Dispose() (lines 70-89) cancels CTSes, detaches subscriptions, but never disposes `_store`; the trailing comment (line 88) "ClipSearchService is owned by the view, disposed there" confirms the store is nobody's disposal responsibility.
5. LibraryView.OnUnloaded (lines 296-325) is the entire teardown path. It disposes ViewModel, `_clip`, `_thumbnails`, and the thumb CTSes — but there is no `store` reference to dispose. LibraryView is `: UserControl, INotifyPropertyChanged` (line 21) and the ONLY partial declaration (grep) — it is NOT IDisposable, so DetailHostView.DisposePriorChild (DetailHostView.cs:159-169), which only disposes children that implement IDisposable, does nothing for it; teardown happens solely via Host.Children.Clear() → Unloaded.
6. Per-navigation recreation is confirmed: DetailHostView.BuildChild (line 133) does `new Library.LibraryView()` for the "library" tab on every swap; SelectionRegistry.cs:5 explicitly notes "the app builds a fresh LibraryViewModel per tab navigation." So each Library round-trip = one new ReadStore + one new open read-only shared-cache connection.

IMPACT (lens): Genuine resource leak. Each open→away→back cycle opens a SqliteConnection (ReadOnly, shared-cache, on the WAL DB) and a SemaphoreSlim that are never Dispose()d. Because ClipSearchService/LibraryViewModel detach their static EngineClient subscriptions on Dispose, the orphaned graph IS eventually GC-collectible, so this is non-deterministic (GC/finalizer-delayed) cleanup, not a permanent unbounded leak — open connections accumulate against the engine's WAL until a GC pass finalizes them. That matches the "medium" severity and the description's wording. The suggested fix (hold the store in a field and Dispose it last in OnUnloaded after ViewModel/_clip, so no in-flight read touches a disposed connection) is correct and consistent with the codebase's existing disposal-order discipline. Confidence is high but not 1.0 only because the harm is GC-recoverable rather than a hard crash/permanent leak.

confirmed.title: AppSettings lost-update: shared static debounce CTS lets a fresh Load().Save() silently discard the long-lived singleton's pending write (and vice versa)
confirmed.description: Settings are written from two disjoint object graphs: the long-lived AppViewModel.Instance.Settings singleton (AppViewModel.cs:29, loaded once and never reloaded — owns ActiveTab/SidebarVisible/LastFolderPath/LastFolderDisplay and is also mutated by SettingsView toggles), and fresh-per-call AppSettings.Load() instances in DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:687, PeopleView.xaml.cs:145, and ModelInstallerService.PersistSelectedVlmModelKind (ModelInstallerService.cs:469). Each Save() snapshots the WHOLE object (CloneForWrite, MemberwiseClone) and serializes the full file. The debounce is keyed on a single static CTS s_pendingSaveCts: Save() does Interlocked.Exchange(ref s_pendingSaveCts, newCts) then cancels the prior CTS (line 250-251), which CANCELS the previously-armed debounced write before it ever touches disk. Because the two object graphs hold independent in-memory state, the later Save's snapshot lacks the earlier object's mutation, so that mutation is permanently lost. s_writeGate only serializes the physical write — it does not prevent the lost update.
confirmed.trigger: User picks a new library folder → AppViewModel.FolderPath setter mutates the singleton's LastFolderPath/LastFolderDisplay and calls _settings.Save() (arms CTS A with a snapshot containing the new folder). Within the 200 ms debounce window the post-clustering auto-chain or the user picking a Deep Analyze model runs DeepAnalyzeView's AppSettings.Load() (reads the OLD on-disk file, without the new folder), sets SelectedVlmModelKind, and calls Save() → Interlocked.Exchange replaces and CANCELS CTS A. CTS A's write never fires; CTS B writes the stale-folder snapshot. The folder pick is silently lost (and symmetrically, a singleton tab/sidebar Save can overwrite a just-persisted VLM/People pick).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Route all persistence through one shared in-memory instance (AppViewModel.Instance.Settings) instead of fresh Load().Save() calls — i.e. have DeepAnalyzeView, PeopleView, and PersistSelectedVlmModelKind mutate the singleton then Save(). If independent Load() callers must remain, make WriteAsync re-read the current on-disk file under s_writeGate and merge only the changed field(s) before writing, rather than blindly overwriting with a stale full snapshot; and do not let a new Save cancel a pending one belonging to a different object (e.g. read-modify-write the file under the gate).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly in source. AppSettings.cs uses a single static debounce CTS `s_pendingSaveCts` (line 242). Every Save() (line 244): builds a fresh CTS, `Interlocked.Exchange(ref s_pendingSaveCts, newCts)` (line 250), then `prior?.Cancel()` (line 251) — which cancels the previously-armed-but-not-yet-fired debounced write before it reaches disk (the worker bails on the cancelled token, lines 257-260). Each Save snapshots the WHOLE object via CloneForWrite()/MemberwiseClone() (lines 252, 286-292) and WriteAsync serializes that full snapshot and unconditionally overwrites the file with File.Move(overwrite:true) (lines 301-305). WriteAsync does NOT re-read or merge — confirmed; s_writeGate (line 297) only serializes the physical write, so it cannot prevent a lost update.

Two disjoint object graphs are confirmed: the long-lived singleton AppViewModel.Instance.Settings (AppViewModel.cs:23/29/39) owns ActiveTab/SidebarVisible/LastFolderPath/LastFolderDisplay (setters call _settings.Save() at lines 50/64/96) and is also mutated by SettingsView toggles (SettingsView.xaml.cs:334/343/358). Independent fresh-per-call AppSettings.Load() instances are created and Saved at DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:687-689, PeopleView.xaml.cs:145-147, and ModelInstallerService.cs:469-472 — none of which touch the singleton.

Lost-update derivation (deterministic, not even requiring true concurrency): Save A (singleton, snapshot contains new folder) arms CTS A. Within the 200ms debounce, Save B from a fresh Load() (snapshot read from the OLD on-disk file, lacking A's folder) runs, cancels CTS A, and arms CTS B. A's deferred write never fires; CTS B writes the stale-folder snapshot. A's mutation is permanently lost. The symmetric case (singleton tab/sidebar Save superseding a just-persisted VLM/People pick) is equally real, and is NOT healed by shutdown SaveImmediately (MainWindow.xaml.cs:563) — that flushes the singleton, which never learned the fresh-instance pick, so it overwrites the pick with the singleton's stale value.

The trigger is realistic: 200ms is a tight debounce; picking a folder then tapping a Deep Analyze model card / a People toggle / an install completion (ModelInstallerService.cs:159) within that window is ordinary interaction, and post-folder-pick programmatic flows fire right after the setter. The CloneForWrite comment (lines 286-291) even documents the snapshot-at-call-time behavior that is the exact lost-update mechanism across instances. Defect is genuinely present, triggerable, and harmful (persisted-settings data loss). Confidence held below 0.9 only because hitting the exact <200ms overlap depends on user timing/auto-chain cadence, but the code defect itself is incontrovertible.

Verified against source. The lost-update mechanism is real and triggerable.

DISJOINT GRAPHS (confirmed): AppViewModel.cs:23-39 — `Instance` is a static-readonly singleton whose `_settings = AppSettings.Load()` runs once at startup and is never reloaded; it owns ActiveTab/SidebarVisible/LastFolderPath/LastFolderDisplay and is mutated by its own setters (lines 50/64/96). Three callers instead do fresh `Load()`→mutate→`Save()` on independent object graphs: DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:687-689 (SelectedVlmModelKind), PeopleView.xaml.cs:145-147 (PeopleHideUnknown), ModelInstallerService.cs:469-472 (SelectedVlmModelKind). (Minor claim correction: SettingsView uses the singleton at line 334, so it is NOT a disjoint caller — but the three above are.)

CANCEL-PRIOR MECHANISM (confirmed): AppSettings.cs:242 `s_pendingSaveCts` is a single static CTS shared by every instance. Save() at 249-251 does `Interlocked.Exchange(ref s_pendingSaveCts, newCts)` then `prior?.Cancel()`. The prior write's `await Task.Delay(SaveDebounce, newCts.Token)` (line 257) throws OperationCanceledException, swallowed at 260, so its `WriteAsync(snapshot)` (258) never executes. Each snapshot is a full MemberwiseClone (286-293) and WriteAsync (295-311) overwrites the whole file with no read-modify-merge.

CONSEQUENCE: If Save A (e.g. singleton FolderPath setter, snapshot has new folder) is in its 200 ms debounce and Save B (e.g. DeepAnalyzeView model pick, fresh Load that read the OLD on-disk file lacking the new folder) fires, B cancels A and writes B's stale snapshot. A's new folder is permanently lost; symmetric the other way.

THREAD/TRIGGER REALITY (the lens): All these Save() calls originate on the UI thread (property setters + RoutedEventArgs handlers). The cancel-prior portion of Save() runs synchronously on the caller, so the second Save deterministically cancels the first's pending delay whenever it lands within the 200 ms window — no off-thread subtlety required. The post-clustering auto-chain / welcome-install flows persist SelectedVlmModelKind via Load()/Save() exactly during the busy post-scan period when tab/folder state is also churning, making a sub-200ms interleave realistic.

NO MASKING: SaveImmediately at shutdown (MainWindow.xaml.cs:563) only flushes the singleton's current state and cannot recover an already-cancelled disjoint write; the loss occurs mid-session.

Severity nuance keeping confidence below ~0.9: the disjoint callers Load() the on-disk file, so only mutations still inside the ≤200ms debounce window (not yet flushed) are lost, not already-persisted values — a real but bounded window. Within that window the loss is silent and the field is gone. Mechanism, trigger, and harm all verified; medium severity is fair.

Verified the mechanism against the actual source and it holds end-to-end.

AppSettings.cs:242 declares a SINGLE static `s_pendingSaveCts` shared by every AppSettings instance. In Save() (244-266): line 250 `Interlocked.Exchange(ref s_pendingSaveCts, newCts)` returns the prior CTS and line 251 `prior?.Cancel()` cancels it. The prior debounced task's `await Task.Delay(SaveDebounce=200ms, newCts.Token)` (257) throws OperationCanceledException, so its `WriteAsync(snapshot)` (258) NEVER runs — the prior snapshot is discarded before any disk write. CloneForWrite() (286-293, MemberwiseClone) captures only the CALLING instance's fields. WriteAsync (295-311) does a blind full-file overwrite (SerializeToUtf8Bytes + File.Move) with NO re-read/merge.

Two disjoint write origins confirmed: the long-lived singleton AppViewModel.Instance.Settings (AppViewModel.cs:29 loaded once; FolderPath setter at 70-99 calls _settings.Save() at 96; ActiveTab/SidebarVisible likewise; SettingsView toggles use AppViewModel.Instance.Settings at SettingsView.xaml.cs:334/343/358) versus fresh-per-call AppSettings.Load()...Save() instances in DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:687-689, PeopleView.xaml.cs:145-147, ModelInstallerService.cs:469-472, and WelcomeSheet.xaml.cs:449. The fresh Load() reads the OLD on-disk file (folder-pick still in the 200ms debounce, not yet flushed), so its snapshot lacks the singleton's mutation; its Save() cancels the singleton's pending write and overwrites with the stale snapshot. The earlier mutation is permanently lost. Symmetric in reverse.

IMPACT lens: this is genuine silent data loss of a persisted user preference (folder pick, tab, VLM model, or People/Settings toggle) — not a crash/hang, not cosmetic, and NOT self-recovered (the cancelled snapshot has no retry path). Triggerable whenever two Save() calls from disjoint object graphs land within the 200ms window (e.g. SettingsView toggle then a PeopleView 'show hidden' tap, or the folder-pick + Deep-Analyze model-pick chain the claim describes). The window is narrow so it's intermittent/low-frequency rather than constant, which fits the 'medium' severity. Cited line numbers are slightly off (DeepAnalyzeView lives under Views/DeepAnalyze/, exact lines 687-689 not 687), but every load-bearing line matches the described logic. Confidence held below 0.9 only because the realistic trigger requires rapid (<200ms) cross-graph saves rather than a single ordinary action.

confirmed.title: FilePreviewSheet image preview and metadata cards have no navigation generation guard — stale async result overwrites the current sibling
confirmed.description: The sheet instance is reused across sibling navigation: SetFile() is called repeatedly on the same control as the user arrow-keys Left/Right (NavigateSibling → SetFile). The media path is fenced against out-of-order completion via the `_mediaGen` token (bumped in StopAndClearMedia on every SetFile, checked in LoadVideoOrAudioAsync at lines 471/490). The image-preview path has NO such fence: LoadShellThumbnailAsync awaits GetThumbnailAsync/SetSourceAsync and then unconditionally assigns `PreviewImage.Source = bmp; PreviewImage.Visibility = Visible;` inside the dispatcher enqueue (lines 359-362) with only an implicit assumption of ordering — it never checks that the file it decoded is still the displayed sibling. TryShowCachedPreview (lines 416-421) likewise enqueues a Source assignment guarded only by `_unloaded`. The proposed-name card (LoadProposedNameAsync, lines 678-681) and Vision Tags card (LoadVisionTagsAsync, lines 788-817) have the same gap — they bind their results guarded only by `_unloaded`, not by a per-navigation token. So a slower decode/DB query for sibling N can resolve AFTER the user has navigated to N+1, leaving N's image / N's proposed name / N's tags shown for file N+1.
confirmed.trigger: Open the preview sheet and arrow-key quickly across image siblings where an earlier file (e.g. a large RAW/HEIC routed through the shell provider, or a cold DB query) decodes/queries more slowly than the next; the earlier file's continuation lands last and the preview/cards display content that doesn't match the file currently selected.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Introduce a single monotonic `_navGen` (int) bumped at the top of SetFile (or in StopAndClearMedia alongside `_mediaGen`), capture it in LoadShellThumbnailAsync / TryShowCachedPreview / LoadProposedNameAsync / LoadVisionTagsAsync before the first await, and inside each dispatcher continuation skip the assignment unless `Volatile.Read(ref _navGen) == captured` — exactly the pattern DeepAnalyzeView.LoadStreamThumbAsync already uses with `_streamThumbGeneration`.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source at platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs and the cited precedent in DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs.

Call chain (UI thread): HandleKeyDown → NavigateSibling (line 168) → SetFile (line 179, async void). SetFile runs synchronously up to its first await, calling StopAndClearMedia (line 203) which bumps only _mediaGen (line 528 — the MEDIA fence), then awaits SetFileCoreAsync (line 218) → for images, LoadShellThumbnailAsync (line 302). Because SetFile is async void and there is NO serialization, rapid Left/Right produces multiple concurrent in-flight LoadShellThumbnailAsync invocations whose completion order is governed by decode latency, not navigation order.

Image path is unfenced as claimed:
- LoadShellThumbnailAsync (line 328) takes no generation, captures none. After awaiting GetFileFromPathAsync (345) and the slow GetThumbnailAsync (346), the TryEnqueue continuation assigns PreviewImage.Source = bmp; Visibility = Visible (lines 361-362) with only a try/catch fast-fail guard — no check that the decoded file is still the current sibling. So a slow RAW/HEIC decode for sibling N can land AFTER the user navigated to N+1, overwriting N+1's image with N's. Confirmed reproducible (slow A → fast/cached B → A's continuation lands last → A clobbers B).
- TryShowCachedPreview continuation (lines 416-421) guards only _unloaded (418), not navigation generation — same gap.
- LoadProposedNameAsync (lines 640-687): off-thread DB query via Task.Run (648) + ConfigureAwait(true); binding (ProposedRenameText.Text / card visibility, 680-681) guarded only by _unloaded (677). ConfigureAwait(true) marshals the continuation to the UI thread but does NOT order independent Task.Run completions, so a cold query for sibling N can bind N's proposed name into N+1's card.
- LoadVisionTagsAsync (lines 743-823): same shape — Task.Run DB read (752), binding (788-817) guarded only by _unloaded (787). FileId is set synchronously at line 182 and the loads kicked off at 194/196, but no continuation re-validates against the current FileId/FilePath.

Contrast confirms intent: DeepAnalyzeView.LoadStreamThumbAsync uses Interlocked.Increment(ref _streamThumbGeneration) (588) and gates every Source write on Volatile.Read(...)==generation (617, 626, 664) for exactly this hazard. The media path here is similarly fenced (gen captured at 306, checked 471/490). The image/proposed-name/vision-tags paths are the only three that lack the fence — an asymmetry that, given the codebase's own established pattern, is a genuine omission, not a misread.

Cited line numbers in the finding are accurate. The defect is genuine, triggerable by quick arrow-key navigation across siblings with uneven decode/query latency, and harmful (wrong image/name/tags shown for the current file). Severity medium is fair (transient wrong-UI-state, self-corrects on re-navigation, no data corruption). Confidence 0.9; minor residual uncertainty only on real-world timing margins for the image race, but the proposed-name/tags races are even easier to hit (cold DB on first open) and the absence of any generation guard is unambiguous in the source.

Verified against FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs and the cited DeepAnalyzeView precedent. The claim holds under the trigger/thread-reality lens.

THREAD/ORDERING REALITY (the core question):
- SetFile is async void (line 179), invoked unawaited from NavigateSibling (line 175) on the UI thread, so rapid Left/Right fires OVERLAPPING SetFile(N) and SetFile(N+1) invocations with NO serialization between them. Confirmed: nothing forces N's load to finish before N+1 starts.
- The ONLY generation token is _mediaGen (lines 458, 471, 490, 528), and it is checked ONLY in LoadVideoOrAudioAsync (the media path). The image path, cached-preview path, proposed-name card, and vision-tags card have no equivalent fence — confirmed by grep (only _mediaGen exists; no _navGen/_previewGen).

IMAGE PATH (lines 328-403): The continuation that assigns PreviewImage.Source = bmp (line 361) is reached only after `await GetFileFromPathAsync` + `await GetThumbnailAsync` (lines 345-346), which the code's own comment (lines 330-335) states "can resume on a worker thread." So the order in which N vs N+1 reach their dispatcher.TryEnqueue is the order their shell thumbnail extraction completes, NOT submission order. A slow RAW/HEIC for N + fast/cached N+1 means N enqueues last and wins. There's a SECOND independent race: inside the enqueued lambda there's another `await bmp.SetSourceAsync` (line 360) before the assignment — even with correct enqueue order, the decode-completion order of the two SetSourceAsync calls decides which PreviewImage.Source write lands last. TryShowCachedPreview (lines 416-421) enqueues a Source assignment guarded only by _unloaded (line 418), so a fast cached N+1 can be overwritten by a slow uncached N. Doubly racy and unguarded — matches the claim.

CARDS (LoadProposedNameAsync lines 640-687, LoadVisionTagsAsync lines 743-823): Each runs its DB read on Task.Run with a FRESH SqliteConnection (lines 653-659, 758-764) — no shared lock, so N and N+1 queries execute concurrently on the thread pool with no completion-order guarantee. ConfigureAwait(true) resumes the continuation on the UI thread, where it commits results guarded only by _unloaded (lines 677-681, 787-817), never by a per-navigation token. A cold query for N completing after N+1's fast query leaves N's name/tags shown on N+1.

HARM (not benign): Worse than cosmetic for the rename card. FileId is set synchronously to N+1 (line 182); the stale continuation sets _pendingProposedName = N's name (line 673). OnApplyRenameClicked builds new RenameEntry(FileId, name!) = (N+1, N's-name) (lines 692-707) and sends it to the engine — i.e., a stale proposed name can be APPLIED to the wrong file. That's a data-integrity hazard.

The suggested fix (a monotonic _navGen captured before the first await and re-checked via Volatile.Read inside each continuation) is exactly the pattern DeepAnalyzeView.LoadStreamThumbAsync already uses with _streamThumbGeneration (verified at lines 32, 588, 616-617, 664), confirming both the precedent and that the FilePreviewSheet paths genuinely lack it.

Confidence held at 0.85 (not higher) because reliably triggering the image-path reorder requires a meaningful decode-latency differential plus very fast arrow-keying, and WinRT continuations sometimes resume inline on the UI thread (reducing the worker-thread reorder window). But the card paths are unambiguously racy (independent thread-pool queries, no ordering), and the rename-apply-to-wrong-file consequence makes this a genuine, triggerable, harmful bug rather than benign.

Verified against C:/Users/adamm/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs (full read) and the reference DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs.

THE BUG IS REAL. Sheet is reused across sibling nav: NavigateSibling (L168-177) calls async-void SetFile (L179) synchronously per arrow-key, not awaited. The image path LoadShellThumbnailAsync (L328-403) is structurally identical to DeepAnalyzeView.LoadStreamThumbAsync (capture dispatcher, await GetThumbnailAsync, enqueue continuation that does `await bmp.SetSourceAsync` then assign Source) EXCEPT it omits the generation guard. FilePreviewSheet commits unconditionally at L361-362; DeepAnalyzeView gates the identical assignment with `Volatile.Read(ref _streamThumbGeneration) == generation` (L616-617). SetFile deliberately leaves the prior image on screen (L198-203 comment; only media/placeholder collapsed), so nothing resets PreviewImage.Source synchronously.

Reordering is genuinely triggerable via two independent mechanisms: (a) the per-continuation `await SetSourceAsync` decode latency differs by file — a large RAW/HEIC for sibling N decodes slower than N+1, so even FIFO-queued continuations commit out of order, leaving N's image on N+1; (b) pre-enqueue GetThumbnailAsync shell-extraction latency differs. The codebase itself documents this exact race (DeepAnalyzeView L27-32) and guards it — the unguarded twin is the bug. TryShowCachedPreview (L416-421) is also only `_unloaded`-guarded and races when cache-hit and slow-extract siblings interleave.

The card paths confirm the rest: LoadProposedNameAsync (L640-687) and LoadVisionTagsAsync (L743-823) are fire-and-forget (L194/196) and guard only `_unloaded` (L677/L787) with no file-id recheck, so a slow DB query for N renders N's name/tags over N+1. Notably this escalates beyond cosmetic for the rename card: `_pendingProposedName` is set to N's name (L673) while `FileId` is synchronously N+1 (L182), so clicking Apply in the stale window (L693) renames N+1 using N's proposed name — a wrong-data write.

IMPACT lens: not a native fast-fail/crash (the BitmapImage is correctly constructed inside the captured dispatcher, so no RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD; not a leak/hang). It is a genuine, reachable wrong-UI-state bug, with a plausible wrong-write escalation on the proposed-name Apply path. No existing mitigation covers it: the deferred-loading timer early-returns once an image is visible (L255-259) and never validates source identity; `_mediaGen` fences only media (L458, L528). Suggested fix (single `_navGen` token, capture before first await, recheck inside each continuation — exactly the `_streamThumbGeneration` pattern) is correct and idiomatic.

Confidence held at 0.88 rather than higher only because the visible-overwrite window is brief (the next correct decode for N+2 would re-correct it on continued navigation, and a user who stops on N+1 sees the wrong content only until nothing further updates), and the wrong-rename escalation additionally requires the user to click Apply during the stale window. But the core wrong-UI-state claim is incontrovertible.

confirmed.title: Sankey "Other" folded-bucket ribbon drills down to the wrong (usually empty) file list
confirmed.description: SankeyFlowControl caps each column at MaxNodes=12 and folds the long tail into a synthetic 'Other' node (SankeyFlowControl.cs lines 171-191, SourceBucket/CategoryBucket). When a ribbon is tapped, RibbonInvoked carries that BUCKET key (which may be the literal string "Other" representing many real folders/categories). RestructureView.OnSankeyRibbonInvoked passes it straight to DrillDownSheet.SetSankeyFilter. But SetSankeyFilter recomputes the bucket with TopLevel() (lines 291-295) which performs NO folding and NO MaxNodes cap, and matches m.Category == category literally (line 63-64). So a tap on the folded 'Other' source ribbon — or the folded 'Other' category ribbon — only matches files literally located in a folder named 'Other' / literally categorized 'Other', not the folded tail the ribbon actually represents. The drill-down shows the wrong content (typically '0 files' or a coincidental subset) instead of the files in that ribbon.
confirmed.trigger: Plan a library with more than 12 distinct top-level source folders (or more than 12 destination categories) so the Sankey folds the tail into an 'Other' node, then tap the 'Other' ribbon. The 'Files in this flow' sheet shows the wrong file list.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the drill-down apply the same bucketing the Sankey used. Either pass the resolved member set from the ribbon hit-test into the sheet, or reproduce SourceBucket/CategoryBucket (incl. MaxNodes fold + 'Other' tail) in SetSankeyFilter so that when source=="Other" it matches every move whose bucket is not in the top-N, and likewise for the category side.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified the full call chain and re-derived independently. The defect is genuine, triggerable, and harmful.

CALL CHAIN:
- SankeyFlowControl.cs:170-191 caps each column at MaxNodes=12 and folds the tail into OtherKey="Other" via SourceBucket (line 178-182) / CategoryBucket (line 187-191), guarded by distinctSources>12 / distinctCats>12.
- Ribbons store Source=src.Key / Category=cat.Key (lines 333-334), which are the BUCKET keys — i.e. the literal string "Other" for a folded ribbon.
- OnTapped fires RibbonInvoked with (h.Source, h.Category) (line 511).
- RestructureView.xaml.cs:186 passes ribbon.Source/ribbon.Category straight into sheet.SetSankeyFilter — no resolution of "Other" into its member set anywhere in between (confirmed: only one SetSankeyFilter call site, "Other"/OtherKey appears only inside SankeyFlowControl).
- DrillDownSheet.xaml.cs:54-69 SetSankeyFilter recomputes srcBucket = TopLevel(srcRel) (line 62; TopLevel at 291-295 is byte-equivalent to SankeyFlowControl.SourceOf but performs NO MaxNodes cap and NO folding) and matches srcBucket==source (line 63) AND m.Category==category literally (line 64).

CONSEQUENCE: A tap on a folded "Other" source ribbon matches only moves whose top-level folder is literally named "Other" (essentially never); a tap on a folded "Other" category ribbon matches only moves whose engine Category is literally "Other". The folded tail the ribbon visually represents is never matched, so the "Files in this flow" sheet shows 0 files (or a coincidental literal-"Other" subset) — wrong UI state. Non-folded ribbons are fine because TopLevel reproduces SourceOf exactly and Category matches literally.

CONFIRMED INTENT via the macOS reference (the visual/behavioral source of truth): RestructureView.swift defines DrillDownScope.sourceFolders(Set)/destBuckets(Set) (lines 1042-1043) matched via Set.contains, and SankeyFlowView.swift taps the rollup node with onTapSourceRollup(layout.rollupSourceFolders) / onTapDestRollup(layout.rollupDestBuckets) (lines 114-118, 139-143) — i.e. the rollup tap deliberately resolves to the member SET of the folded tail. The Windows port dropped that resolution, so it is a true port-fidelity regression, not intended behavior.

TRIGGERABILITY: realistic — a messy library with 13+ top-level source folders, or 13+ destination categories (the People/<name> buckets plus Documents/Places/Photos-by-year etc. can exceed 12), triggers the fold.

Lens (logic): not a misread. The marshaling/threading is irrelevant here; the defect is pure data-flow — the bucket label is passed where the bucket member-set is needed, and the sheet re-derives buckets without the fold. Minor report inaccuracy (cosmetic only): the file lives at Views/Restructure/SankeyFlowControl.cs, not Controls/; all cited line numbers and the logic are otherwise accurate.

Verified the full call chain and re-derived the logic independently.

CALL CHAIN (all on the UI thread — no thread concern invalidates it):
- SankeyFlowControl.OnTapped (SankeyFlowControl.cs:502-513) hit-tests a Ribbon and fires RibbonInvoked?.Invoke(this, (h.Source, h.Category)) at line 511.
- Each Ribbon's Source/Category are bucket keys: at lines 330-339 Source = src.Key / Category = cat.Key, where src/cat come from bySource/byCategory, which are built from SourceBucket/CategoryBucket. SourceBucket (lines 178-182) returns the literal OtherKey "Other" when distinctSources > MaxNodes(12) and the source is not in topSources; CategoryBucket (lines 187-191) does the same for categories. So a folded ribbon literally carries Source=="Other" (or Category=="Other").
- RestructureView.OnSankeyRibbonInvoked (RestructureView.xaml.cs:181-196) passes ribbon.Source/ribbon.Category straight through: sheet.SetSankeyFilter(plan, ribbon.Source, ribbon.Category) at line 186. No member set is passed.
- DrillDownSheet.SetSankeyFilter (DrillDownSheet.xaml.cs:54-70) recomputes srcBucket = TopLevel(srcRel) (line 62). TopLevel (lines 291-295) returns parts[0] (the actual top-level folder name) — it performs NO MaxNodes cap and NO "Other" folding. The match is srcBucket == source (line 63) AND m.Category == category (line 64), literal compares.

CONSEQUENCE: When the folded "Other" source ribbon is tapped, source=="Other". The filter then matches only moves whose actual top-level folder is literally named "Other" — NOT the folded tail (folders ranked outside the top 11). The tail folders have their own names as TopLevel, so they never match. The sheet shows "0 files" (or, by coincidence, only files physically located in a folder literally named "Other"). The category axis has the identical defect: for the folded "Other" category ribbon, line 64 matches only moves whose literal Category is "Other", missing the folded category tail.

TRIGGER REALITY: The "Other" node is rendered as an ordinary ribbon (the draw loop at lines 305-341 iterates bySource/byCategory including the "Other" bucket), so it is hit-testable and tappable. Folding triggers whenever distinctSources > 12 or distinctCats > 12 — a realistic condition for libraries with many top-level folders / destination categories. This is exactly the claimed trigger.

This is a genuine correctness / wrong-UI-state bug, fully reachable on the UI thread, with the described harmful effect (wrong, typically empty, file list). Confidence is 0.9 rather than higher only because the exact severity of the visible result depends on whether any move is coincidentally in/categorized "Other"; in the common case it is plainly wrong (empty). The suggested fix (reproduce the SourceBucket/CategoryBucket fold in SetSankeyFilter, or pass the resolved member set) is correct.

Verified the full call chain and re-derived the bucketing logic independently; the claim holds.

WHAT THE SANKEY DOES: SankeyFlowControl.cs lines 172-191 cap each column at MaxNodes=12 and fold the tail into the literal string OtherKey="Other". SourceBucket(m) returns "Other" when distinctSources>12 and the source isn't in topSources (top 11 by count); CategoryBucket(m) does the same for categories. Groups are keyed on these bucketed strings (lines 193-201), and each Ribbon stores Source=src.Key / Category=cat.Key (lines 330-339) — i.e. the bucketed value, which can be "Other". OnTapped fires RibbonInvoked with (h.Source, h.Category) (line 511), so tapping a folded ribbon emits literal "Other".

HANDOFF: RestructureView.OnSankeyRibbonInvoked (RestructureView.xaml.cs:181-196) passes ribbon.Source / ribbon.Category straight into sheet.SetSankeyFilter (line 186) with no un-folding.

WHAT THE DRILL-DOWN DOES: DrillDownSheet.SetSankeyFilter (lines 59-68) recomputes srcBucket = TopLevel(srcRel). TopLevel (lines 291-295) only splits the relative path and returns parts[0] or "(root)" — it performs NO MaxNodes cap and NO fold to "Other". It then matches srcBucket==source (line 63) and m.Category==category (line 64) literally. RestructureMove.Source/Category are plain unnormalized strings (CommandPayload.cs:69-71).

CONSEQUENCE: When the tapped ribbon's key is the synthetic "Other", SetSankeyFilter only collects moves whose actual top-level folder is literally named "Other" (source side) or whose engine category string is literally "Other" (category side) — essentially never the folded tail the ribbon represents. Result is the wrong list, almost always "0 files" or a coincidental unrelated subset. This exactly matches the claimed wrong-UI-state harm.

IMPACT LENS: This is not a crash/hang/leak/data-loss — it's a deterministic, triggerable correctness/wrong-UI defect, consistent with the stated medium severity and "correctness / wrong UI state" category. The trigger is accurate: >12 distinct top-level source folders OR >12 destination categories causes the fold (the distinctSources>MaxNodes / distinctCats>MaxNodes guards), after which tapping the "Other" ribbon misrenders the sheet. Confidence is high (0.93 rather than 1.0) only because manifestation requires the fold condition to actually be met on a real plan and because a library that happens to contain a folder literally named "Other" would show a partially-coincidental list rather than empty — but in all those cases the displayed content is still wrong relative to what the ribbon represents, so the bug stands.

confirmed.title: SpringEasing.AnimateScale anchors scale at the visual's top-left, not its center (visual.Size is unpopulated (0,0))
confirmed.description: AnimateScale sets the scale center via `var size = visual.Size; visual.CenterPoint = new Vector3(size.X/2, size.Y/2, 0);` where `visual` comes from `ElementCompositionPreview.GetElementVisual(element)`. The visual returned by GetElementVisual does NOT have its Size auto-synced to the element's layout bounds — Size stays (0,0) unless the caller explicitly sets it or binds it via an ExpressionAnimation. So CenterPoint is computed as (0,0) and the spring scales the element from its top-left corner, not symmetrically around its center. The codebase already knows this: LibraryView.AnimateTileEntry (LibraryView.xaml.cs:546-555) reads `visual.Size`, and when `size.X <= 0 || size.Y <= 0` it bails with the comment "a corner-anchored scale would look wrong", then sets CenterPoint itself from a known-good size before calling AnimateScale. Call sites that rely solely on AnimateScale do NOT pre-set CenterPoint and therefore get the broken corner-anchored scale.
confirmed.trigger: Open the Restructure tab and hover the 'Apply (symlink)' or 'Apply (moves)' button, or hover the Keep/Tidy/Reorganize outcome tiles. RestructureView.xaml.cs:88/91/95/98 and :479 call SpringEasing.AnimateScale(button/tile, 1.02f/1.012f, ...) with no prior CenterPoint set; visual.Size is (0,0), so CenterPoint is (0,0) and the control grows/shrinks from its top-left corner (shifting right/down) instead of scaling in place — a visibly wrong hover spring on every Apply-button/tile hover.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Do not derive the center from visual.Size (which is unreliable for GetElementVisual). Instead read the element's layout size, e.g. `if (element is FrameworkElement fe) visual.CenterPoint = new Vector3((float)fe.ActualWidth/2, (float)fe.ActualHeight/2, 0);`, or set RelativeSizePercent / bind the visual's Size to the element. Alternatively set `visual.RelativeOffsetAdjustment`/use `ElementCompositionPreview.SetIsTranslationEnabled` patterns, but the simplest correct fix is to anchor CenterPoint from ActualWidth/ActualHeight (guarding the pre-layout 0 case as AnimateTileEntry already does).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against actual source (paths differ slightly from the claim but the code is present).

CORE DEFECT CONFIRMED — SpringEasing.cs:88-93 (AnimateScale): `var visual = ElementCompositionPreview.GetElementVisual(element); var size = visual.Size; visual.CenterPoint = new Vector3(size.X/2, size.Y/2, 0);`. A `GetElementVisual` visual does NOT auto-sync `Size` to layout bounds — Size stays (0,0) unless set explicitly or bound via ExpressionAnimation. So CenterPoint computes to (0,0) and the spring anchors scale at the top-left corner.

THE CODEBASE ITSELF CONFIRMS Size is (0,0): LibraryView.xaml.cs:546-556 reads `var size = visual.Size` and guards `if (size.X <= 0 || size.Y <= 0)` with the comment "Pre-layout: a corner-anchored scale would look wrong" — direct internal acknowledgment. The only places `Size` is ever populated are LavaLampBackground.cs (self-created child visuals, `_root.Size = ...`), never the elements passed to AnimateScale.

CORRECT PATTERN EXISTS ELSEWHERE (proving the bug by contrast): ApplyTileScale (LibraryView.xaml.cs:720-728) sets `visual.CenterPoint` from `el.ActualWidth/ActualHeight` and bypasses AnimateScale entirely; LibraryView:569 pre-sets CenterPoint from a known-good size before calling AnimateScale. Callers that care work around the helper.

BROKEN CALL SITES REAL + REACHABLE: RestructureView.xaml.cs:88/91/95/98 (Apply-symlink / Apply-moves button PointerEntered/Exited, scale 1.02f) and :479 (UpdateTileHighlight → Keep/Tidy/Reorg outcome tiles, scale 1.012f) call SpringEasing.AnimateScale with no prior CenterPoint set. On hover, Size==(0,0) ⇒ CenterPoint==(0,0) ⇒ control scales from top-left (shifts right/down) instead of in place. Cosmetic but visible, hence the medium severity is right.

Only inaccuracy is minor line-number/path drift (cited :89 vs actual 88-93; Views/Restructure/ vs Views/). Substance is fully accurate and the suggested fix (CenterPoint from ActualWidth/ActualHeight, guarding pre-layout 0) is exactly what the working call sites already do. Confidence 0.9; the WinUI Composition Size behavior is standard and corroborated by the project's own guard code.

Verified against source. SpringEasing.AnimateScale (FileID.Theme/Motion/SpringEasing.cs:82-94) computes CenterPoint from `visual.Size` where `visual = ElementCompositionPreview.GetElementVisual(element)` (lines 88-91). The hand-out Visual returned by GetElementVisual is not auto-synced to layout, so its Size stays (0,0) unless explicitly populated — making CenterPoint (0,0) and anchoring the scale at the element's top-left, not its center.

The codebase itself confirms this premise in THREE independent places:
1) LavaLampBackground.cs:100,151 manually sets `_root.Size = new Vector2((float)ActualWidth, ...)` because the composition visual's Size is not free.
2) LibraryView.AnimateTileEntry (Views/Library/LibraryView.xaml.cs:546-556) reads `visual.Size`, bails when `size.X <= 0 || size.Y <= 0` with the comment "a corner-anchored scale would look wrong," then pre-sets `visual.CenterPoint` at line 569 before calling AnimateScale.
3) LibraryView.ApplyTileScale (lines 720-729) — the correct hover-scale pattern — sets CenterPoint from `el.ActualWidth/2, el.ActualHeight/2` (723-724) and never trusts visual.Size. This is exactly the suggested fix.

Trigger/thread reality (the verification lens): the RestructureView call sites do NOT pre-set CenterPoint and rely solely on AnimateScale: WireApplyBarHoverSprings (Views/Restructure/RestructureView.xaml.cs:81-99, wired at line 69) calls AnimateScale on the Apply buttons at lines 88/91/95/98; tile hover routes PointerEntered → OnKeepTileEntered/OnTidyTileEntered/OnReorgTileEntered (447-449) → SetHoveredOutcome (462) → UpdateTileHighlight → AnimateScale at line 479. All are ordinary UI-thread pointer events that fire on every hover — fully reachable, no thread/lifecycle escape, no declarative CenterPoint/RenderTransformOrigin in the XAML, and no global Size-binding or RelativeSizeAdjustment anywhere in the app to compensate.

So the spring scales these controls from their top-left corner (shifting right/down) rather than symmetrically. Genuine, triggerable, and visible. Confidence not 1.0 only because the visual magnitude is modest (1.02x / 1.012x), so "medium" severity is slightly generous (closer to low-medium), but the bug itself is unambiguous and the suggested fix (anchor CenterPoint from ActualWidth/ActualHeight, guarding the pre-layout 0 case) is correct and already used elsewhere in the same codebase.

Verified against actual source. SpringEasing.AnimateScale (SpringEasing.cs:82-94) derives the scale anchor from `var size = visual.Size; visual.CenterPoint = new Vector3(size.X/2, size.Y/2, 0)` where `visual = ElementCompositionPreview.GetElementVisual(element)` (line 88-91) — exactly as claimed. The handout visual from GetElementVisual does NOT auto-sync its Size to the XAML element's layout bounds; Size stays (0,0) unless explicitly set or bound via ExpressionAnimation, so CenterPoint becomes (0,0) and the spring scales from the top-left corner.

The codebase corroborates this in two places, both of which deliberately avoid trusting AnimateScale's internal Size read: (1) AnimateTileEntry (LibraryView.xaml.cs:546-556) reads visual.Size, guards `size.X <= 0 || size.Y <= 0`, and bails with the literal comment "a corner-anchored scale would look wrong" — proving the team observed (0,0) Size producing corner-anchored scale; it pre-sets CenterPoint itself at line 569 from a verified-nonzero size before calling AnimateScale. (2) ApplyTileScale (LibraryView.xaml.cs:720-728), the directly analogous HOVER path, bypasses AnimateScale entirely and sets `visual.CenterPoint = new Vector3((float)(el.ActualWidth/2), (float)(el.ActualHeight/2), 0)` from layout size — which is precisely the claim's suggested fix, with a comment (703-705) that CenterPoint must be reset per hover to scale around the actual center.

The Restructure call sites confirmed reachable and broken: WireApplyBarHoverSprings (RestructureView.xaml.cs:85-98) calls AnimateScale(ApplySymlinkButton/ApplyMovesButton, 1.02f, ...) on PointerEntered/Exited with no prior CenterPoint; UpdateTileHighlight (line 479) calls AnimateScale(tile, 1.012f/1.0f, ...) on hover via SetHoveredOutcome. The XAML (RestructureView.xaml:210/229/248 Borders, 662/680 Buttons) sets no Composition CenterPoint or Size binding. Hover fires post-layout but visual.Size is still not auto-populated, so CenterPoint=(0,0) → top-left-anchored scale, shifting the control toward bottom-right instead of growing in place.

Under the IMPACT lens: this is purely cosmetic — no crash, no fast-fail, no thread issue, no leak, no data loss. The claim correctly classifies it medium / theme-rendering and does NOT overstate it as a crash. The defect is real and triggerable; the only mitigating nuance is that the scale factors are tiny (1.02x / 1.012x), so the corner-anchoring drift is a few pixels (~1-3px) — perceptible as a slight rightward/downward nudge rather than a clean symmetric scale, but minor. That makes the claimed harm (a visibly-wrong, corner-anchored hover spring) genuine but small in magnitude. Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) because the exact runtime value of visual.Size for these specific element types in the shipped WinUI App SDK version is not provably (0,0) by static reading alone — but the codebase's own defensive guards, comments, and the deliberate ActualWidth/Height fix in the parallel hover path are strong, consistent evidence that the team treats visual.Size as unreliable and that the corner-anchor defect is what actually happens. Minor file-path imprecision in the claim (actual paths include /Restructure/ and /Library/ subdirs; line refs essentially correct) does not affect the verdict.

confirmed.title: WaitFor* / Deep-Analyze continuations write EngineClient observable state off the UI thread, firing PropertyChanged on a thread-pool thread
confirmed.description: Several awaited-bounded command helpers resolve their TaskCompletionSource via a PropertyChanged handler that runs on the UI thread, but the TCS is created with RunContinuationsAsynchronously and the helper resumes with `await tcs.Task.ConfigureAwait(false)` — so the code after the await runs on a thread-pool thread. On that pool thread they then write EngineClient observable properties directly: DeepAnalyzeFileAsync sets `LastWarning` (line 433) and on timeout (line 442); RestoreFromTrashAsync sets `LastWarning`/`LastError` (lines 732, 740, 748). Each write goes through `Set` (EngineClient.cs:1294) which invokes `PropertyChanged` synchronously on that pool thread. EngineClient.Set — unlike ModelSlot.Set, which captures the dispatcher and marshals (ModelSlot.cs:303-324) — does NOT marshal, so every EngineClient.PropertyChanged subscriber is dispatched off the UI thread for these events. This violates the documented invariant in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md ('post XAML writes through DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue ... a naked handler that touches a DispatcherObject is a native fast-fail'). Today every subscriber re-marshals (SidebarEngineStatus/SidebarProcessingControl/DeepAnalyzeView/ModelInstallerService all TryEnqueue or route through ModelSlot), so no crash is reproducible right now — hence low severity — but LastWarning/LastError are even publicly settable and the next subscriber added without a TryEnqueue would fast-fail intermittently and unreproducibly. Treat as a latent variant of the V15.2 class.
confirmed.trigger: Deep-Analyze a single file that the engine fails (Failed>0) or that times out, or restore-from-trash with a partial failure: the resulting LastWarning/LastError write executes on a ThreadPool thread, raising PropertyChanged off the UI thread to every EngineClient subscriber.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Marshal these off-thread observable writes through the captured dispatcher: wrap the post-await `LastWarning = ...`/`LastError = ...` in `_ui.TryEnqueue(() => ...)` (as StartPrewarmStallGuardAsync already does at EngineClient.Commands.cs:604), or make EngineClient.Set marshal via `_ui` exactly like ModelSlot.Set so the UI-thread guarantee holds for all callers regardless of continuation thread.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified every link of the claimed chain against the actual source.

MECHANICS CONFIRMED:
1. EngineClient.Commands.cs:408 — DeepAnalyzeFileAsync creates the TCS with TaskCreationOptions.RunContinuationsAsynchronously, and line 430 awaits it with `await tcs.Task.ConfigureAwait(false)`. Identical pattern in WaitForBulkActionResultAsync (line 355 + 382), which RestoreFromTrashAsync awaits at line 724-727 with ConfigureAwait(false).
2. The TCS is resolved by a PropertyChanged handler (lines 410-417 / 357-367) that fires from the DeepAnalyzeComplete / LastBulkAction setters. Those setters route through Set (EngineClient.cs:251-254, 286-290) which raises PropertyChanged synchronously (EngineClient.cs:1301). PropertyChanged is raised from Apply(ev), and Apply is dispatched onto the UI thread via `_ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev))` (EngineClient.cs:705). So the handler + tcs.TrySetResult run on the UI thread.
3. Because the TCS uses RunContinuationsAsynchronously, the continuation is NOT run inline on the completing (UI) thread; combined with ConfigureAwait(false) (no captured SynchronizationContext), the post-await code resumes on a thread-pool thread. This is correct .NET continuation semantics.
4. The post-await writes execute on that pool thread: LastWarning at line 433 and line 442 (DeepAnalyzeFileAsync), and LastWarning/LastError at lines 732, 740, 748 (RestoreFromTrashAsync). LastError/LastWarning route through Set (EngineClient.cs:209, 219).
5. EngineClient.Set (lines 1294-1302) raises PropertyChanged?.Invoke synchronously with NO dispatcher marshaling — confirmed by direct read. This is in direct contrast to ModelSlot.Set (Services/ModelSlot.cs:303-324), which checks `_ui.HasThreadAccess` and marshals via `_ui.TryEnqueue` precisely to avoid RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD / COMException 0x8001010E (documented in the comment at lines 307-312, citing a real prior crash in the welcome sheet).

So the defect is genuinely present as written: for the Failed>0 and timeout paths, the EngineClient.PropertyChanged event for LastWarning/LastError IS raised off the UI thread.

WHY NOT REFUTED / SEVERITY CALIBRATION: The finder is honest that no crash reproduces today because every current subscriber re-marshals (CLAUDE.md documents the SafeRun + TryEnqueue invariant; SidebarEngineStatus/DeepAnalyzeView/etc. all enqueue). I did not exhaustively audit every subscriber, but the finder concedes this and classifies it low/latent, which is consistent. The bug is real and triggerable (the PropertyChanged event truly fires off-thread), it violates the documented platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md invariant ("never construct DispatcherObject-derived types on a thread you didn't capture ... a naked handler is a native fast-fail"), and LastWarning is even publicly settable. The harm is latent rather than active, so it is a correctly-rated LOW-severity latent bug of the V15.2 class, with a feasible fix (`_ui` is a captured DispatcherQueue at EngineClient.cs:51/368, and StartPrewarmStallGuardAsync at line 604 already uses `_ui.TryEnqueue` for exactly this kind of off-thread LastError write — establishing precedent within the same file).

Confidence 0.82 rather than higher because "harmful" under a strict lens depends on a future subscriber being added without a TryEnqueue; today it is benign. But the mechanical defect (off-UI-thread PropertyChanged on these specific writes, against a documented invariant and inconsistent with the sibling ModelSlot.Set and the same file's own line-604 precedent) is incontrovertibly present.

Verified every claim against source; all check out.

CONTINUATION-THREAD TRACE (the core of the claim): In DeepAnalyzeFileAsync the TCS is created with TaskCreationOptions.RunContinuationsAsynchronously (EngineClient.Commands.cs:408) and resumed via `await tcs.Task.ConfigureAwait(false)` (line 430). The handler that completes the TCS runs on the UI thread (Apply dispatches PropertyChanged), but RunContinuationsAsynchronously forces the awaiter's continuation OFF the completing thread, and ConfigureAwait(false) means no SynchronizationContext recapture — so the code after line 430 runs on a thread-pool thread. The Failed>0 branch then sets `LastWarning =` on line 433 from that pool thread. The TimeoutException path (line 442) fires from cts.Token.Register's callback context (also not the UI thread). RestoreFromTrashAsync (line 720) does the same: `await WaitForBulkActionResultAsync(...).ConfigureAwait(false)` (lines 724-727; that helper's TCS is also RunContinuationsAsynchronously at line 355 and awaited with ConfigureAwait(false) at line 382), then writes `LastWarning =` (732), `LastError =` (740, 748) on the pool continuation.

OFF-THREAD PROPERTYCHANGED CONFIRMED: LastWarning (EngineClient.cs:216-220) and LastError (206-210) both route through Set (1294-1302), which calls `PropertyChanged?.Invoke(this, ...)` synchronously with NO dispatcher marshalling. Contrast ModelSlot.Set (ModelSlot.cs:303-324) which checks `_ui.HasThreadAccess` and TryEnqueues — exactly as the claim states, line numbers match. So for these specific writes every EngineClient.PropertyChanged subscriber is invoked off the UI thread, violating the documented invariant in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md.

CODEBASE OWN AWARENESS: StartPrewarmStallGuardAsync already marshals an off-thread LastError write via `_ui.TryEnqueue(() => LastError = ...)` (Commands.cs:604); _ui is the UI dispatcher captured at ctor (cs:51, 368). This is the correct pattern the buggy sites omit.

LOW-SEVERITY / NO-CRASH-TODAY ASSESSMENT IS ACCURATE: I traced every LastError/LastWarning subscriber. SidebarEngineStatus re-marshals (TryEnqueue, :50), SidebarProcessingControl re-marshals (:65,:70), RestructureView re-marshals (:152), ModelInstallerService.HandleEngineError early-returns for non-install kinds (restore_*/deep_analyze_* are not install errors, :858-870) and where it writes it routes through ModelSlot which marshals internally (explicit comment :875-876). So nothing fast-fails today — but the events DO fire off-thread, and LastWarning is publicly settable (:216), so a future naked subscriber would intermittently/unreproducibly native-fast-fail. This is a genuine, triggerable (Deep-Analyze a single failing/timed-out file; restore-from-trash with partial failure), correctly-located latent thread-affinity invariant violation of the V15.2 class. Confidence 0.9 (slightly short of 1.0 only because the harm is latent, not a live crash — consistent with the claim's own 'low' severity).

The threading MECHANISM described is accurate, but under the IMPACT lens this is a latent invariant smell with no triggerable harm today, so it is refuted as a live bug.

What is true (verified):
- DeepAnalyzeFileAsync (EngineClient.Commands.cs:408-451) and WaitForBulkActionResultAsync (352-388, used by RestoreFromTrashAsync 720-751) create TCS with RunContinuationsAsynchronously and resume via `await tcs.Task.ConfigureAwait(false)` (lines 430, 382). The TCS is resolved inside a PropertyChanged handler that runs on the UI thread (415/365), or on timeout via cts.Token.Register. With ConfigureAwait(false) + RunContinuationsAsynchronously the continuation is posted to a thread-pool thread, so the post-await writes LastWarning (433/442/732) and LastError (740/748) execute off the UI thread.
- EngineClient.Set (EngineClient.cs:1294-1302) fires PropertyChanged synchronously with NO marshaling, unlike ModelSlot.Set (ModelSlot.cs:303-324) which captures _ui and TryEnqueues. LastWarning's setter is indeed public (216-219). So EngineClient.PropertyChanged genuinely fires off-thread for these events. All claim mechanics check out.

Why it is NOT a real/harmful bug today (the decisive part):
- Every current EngineClient.PropertyChanged subscriber that acts on LastError/LastWarning re-marshals before touching any XAML/DispatcherObject: SidebarProcessingControl TryEnqueue(Sync)/TryEnqueue(SyncWarningBanner) (xaml.cs:65/70), SidebarEngineStatus TryEnqueue(Sync) (xaml.cs:50), RestructureView TryEnqueue(...SyncEngineError) (xaml.cs:152), and ModelInstallerService.HandleEngineError which (a) returns early for any non-install kind at line 870 — restore_partial_failure/restore_no_confirm/restore_failed/deep_analyze_file_failed/deep_analyze_no_confirm are never install kinds — and (b) even when it acts, routes through ModelSlot.Set which marshals (with an explicit comment at 875-876 noting it is safe off-thread).
- No XAML binds EngineClient.LastError/LastWarning directly. The x:Bind ...LastError occurrences in SettingsView.xaml and WelcomeSheet.xaml all target ModelSlot.LastError (Svc.Clip/Arcface/RamPlus/DeepVlm/Accelerator.LastError), which marshals.
- Therefore no DispatcherObject is touched on the pool thread; no RPC_E_WRONG_THREAD native fast-fail, hang, leak, or data loss is reachable. This is a tolerated cross-thread PropertyChanged raise that all consumers defensively re-marshal.

The claim itself concedes "no crash is reproducible right now — hence low severity" and asks to treat it as a latent variant. Under the IMPACT lens (real fast-fail vs. tolerated cross-thread access), it is the latter: benign today. The suggested fix (wrap in _ui.TryEnqueue like line 604, or make EngineClient.Set marshal like ModelSlot.Set) is reasonable defensive hardening of a documented invariant, but the bug as a triggerable harm is refuted. Confidence is not higher only because the off-thread PropertyChanged raise does technically violate the documented CLAUDE.md invariant, so a reviewer could legitimately classify it as a real (if low-severity) invariant violation rather than purely cosmetic.

confirmed.title: DeepAnalyzeView _proposedNameCount is never reset at the start of a new run — over-states pending smart-rename count
confirmed.description: _proposedNameCount is incremented (line 418) for every DeepAnalyzeLast event that carries a ProposedName, and the pill text renders 'N smart names pending rename'. The only resets are clicking the pill when the DB reports zero pending (line 524) and refreshing from the DB after the BulkRenameSheet dialog closes (line 560). It is never reset when a new Deep Analyze run begins (no reset on DeepAnalyzeStarting/DeepAnalyzeComplete, and the EngineClient.DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent arm only clears DeepAnalyzeComplete, not this view-local field). So a second 'Analyze All' in the same session keeps accumulating onto the prior run's tally, over-stating the count. (It also under-counts within a run because DeepAnalyzeLast is throttled to 2 Hz in EngineClient, dropping intermediate per-file results.) The displayed number self-corrects only once the user opens the pill, which re-queries the DB.
confirmed.trigger: Run 'Analyze All', let it produce several proposed names, then run 'Analyze All' (or analyze a selection) a second time in the same session; the pill shows the sum of both runs' proposed names rather than the actual pending count.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Reset `_proposedNameCount = 0` when a fresh run starts — e.g. in the DeepAnalyzeStarting branch of SyncStream (when starting is non-null and prog is null) or in OnAnalyzeAllClicked/OnAnalyzeSelectedClicked before the await — so the pill reflects only the current run, or drive the pill purely from a DB count query rather than an event-incremented field.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual source. DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs: `_proposedNameCount` is declared at line 338 (default 0); the ONLY mutations are the increment at line 418 (inside the `last is not null` arm of SyncStream when `last.ProposedName` is non-empty) and the two DB-driven resets at line 524 (pill click, DB count == 0) and line 560 (after the BulkRenameSheet dialog closes). There is no reset anywhere else: not in OnAnalyzeAllClicked (lines 721-748), not in OnAnalyzeSelectedClicked (753-765), not in any view-lifecycle handler.

Cross-checked the engine-side lifecycle in EngineClient.cs: the DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent arm (lines 1139-1148) sets DeepAnalyzeStarting and clears ONLY DeepAnalyzeComplete = null — it does NOT clear DeepAnalyzeLast and obviously cannot touch the view-local _proposedNameCount. DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent (1162-1165) clears Progress + Starting but not Last. StartScanAsync (Commands.cs:100-102) clears Starting/Progress/Complete but is a scan path and also leaves Last untouched. confirmed.title: SqliteConnection leaked (never disposed/closed) in the hidden-unknowns footer query
confirmed.description: The SqliteConnection created at line 94 is opened (line 100) but never wrapped in `using` and never Closed/Disposed — only the SqliteCommand is `using`-disposed. Every UpdateHiddenUnknownsFooter call (OnLoaded, OnToggleHiddenUnknowns, each manual refresh) opens a new read-only connection to the DB and abandons it; it returns to the Microsoft.Data.Sqlite pool only on GC finalization. Contrast the sibling read paths which are correct: QueryCaptionedFraction (RestructureView.cs:546) and PersonDetailSheet.Load (PersonDetailSheet.cs:96) both use `using var conn`. A lingering read connection on the WAL DB can briefly hold a read lock and delay the engine's single-writer WAL checkpoint, and accumulates open file handles until GC.
confirmed.trigger: Open the People tab and refresh repeatedly (e.g. run face clustering, toggle 'Show hidden', re-cluster). Each pass abandons one open SQLite connection until the GC finalizer runs.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change line 94 to `using var conn = new Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteConnection(...)` so the connection is deterministically returned to the pool when the Task.Run lambda exits, matching QueryCaptionedFraction and PersonDetailSheet.Load.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual source at platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/People/PeopleView.xaml.cs (note: cited as PeopleView.xaml.cs, the line numbers match). In UpdateHiddenUnknownsFooter (lines 83-134): line 94 creates `var conn = new Microsoft.Data.Sqlite.SqliteConnection(...)` as a plain local (NOT `using`); line 100 calls `conn.Open()`; line 101 is `using var cmd = conn.CreateCommand()` — only the command is disposed. The lambda returns at line 104 (or 106 in catch) with no `conn.Close()`, `conn.Dispose()`, `try/finally`, or enclosing `using` for `conn` anywhere in the method. SqliteConnection is disposable/finalizable, so the underlying handle returns to the Microsoft.Data.Sqlite pool only on GC finalization — one leaked open read-only connection per invocation. The thread context (Task.Run pool thread) does not save it: nothing disposes the connection on any thread. Trigger is real and reachable: UpdateHiddenUnknownsFooter is invoked from OnLoadedAsync (line 74), OnToggleHiddenUnknowns/"Show hidden" tap (line 152), and after refresh/clustering passes — each call leaks one. The two contrast paths cited by the finder are exact: RestructureView.xaml.cs:546 is `using var conn = new ...SqliteConnection(...)` and PersonDetailSheet.xaml.cs:96 is `using var conn = new SqliteConnection(connStr)` — both correct, confirming this is the outlier. Severity "low" is appropriate (read-only WAL connection, GC eventually reclaims it), and the suggested fix (change line 94 to `using var conn`) is correct and matches the established sibling pattern. Minor caveat (-0.05): impact is bounded — connection pooling and GC mean it is a transient/handle-pressure leak rather than an unbounded one, and the read-lock/WAL-checkpoint delay is real but brief.

Verified against the actual source. At platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/People/PeopleView.xaml.cs:94 a SqliteConnection is created, opened at line 100, but only the SqliteCommand is using-wrapped (line 101). The conn local goes out of scope at line 107 (the Task.Run lambda's return) with no Dispose/Close and no try/finally; nothing else references it (confirmed by reading the full method, lines 83-134, and the class — no field holds it). So the connection is genuinely abandoned undisposed.

Pooling does NOT rescue this. Microsoft.Data.Sqlite is pinned at 9.0.0 (Directory.Packages.props:40), where pooling is on by default, but a connection is returned to the pool ONLY on Dispose/Close — abandoning it leaves the native SQLite handle (and its OS file handle on the WAL DB) open until the GC finalizer runs SqliteConnection's finalizer. The claim's stated mechanism ("returns to the pool only on GC finalization; accumulates open file handles until GC") is therefore technically accurate.

Trigger is real and off no special thread constraints: UpdateHiddenUnknownsFooter is invoked from OnLoadedAsync (line 74) and OnToggleHiddenUnknowns (line 140+), and the DB work runs on a thread-pool thread via Task.Run (line 89). Each invocation abandons one open connection.

Two framing inaccuracies that lower confidence but do not refute the defect: (1) The claim's "contrast the correct sibling read paths" is selective — RestructureView.xaml.cs:546, PersonDetailSheet.xaml.cs:96, PeopleViewModel.cs:161, CleanupViewModel.cs:121 use `using`, but the IDENTICAL no-using pattern also appears at SettingsView.xaml.cs:87, DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:143, FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs:653 and :758. So it is a pervasive house style, not a lone deviation. (2) The WAL-checkpoint-delay impact is overstated: the COUNT(*) statement is finalized (using cmd disposes at line 107) before the lambda returns, so the lingering connection holds no active read transaction and won't meaningfully block the single-writer checkpoint; the real residual cost is leaked OS file handles + native memory + finalization pressure until GC.

Net: a genuine, triggerable, undisposed-connection resource leak with no cleanup path. Severity low is correct and the suggested fix (`using var conn`) is the right one. The defect stands despite the imperfect framing.

VERIFIED FACTS (read PeopleView.xaml.cs:83-134): line 94 does create `var conn = new SqliteConnection(...)` without `using`; conn.Open() at line 100; only `cmd` is `using`-disposed (line 101); the Task.Run lambda returns (line 104/106) without ever Closing/Disposing `conn`. So the literal "no `using` on the connection" observation is accurate.

WHY IT IS NOT THE BUG AS CLAIMED, under the IMPACT lens:

1) FRAMING IS WRONG. The claim asserts this is an outlier vs. "correct" siblings. Grep across FileID.App shows the OPPOSITE: this exact pattern (`var conn = new SqliteConnection(...); conn.Open(); using var cmd = ...`, no `using` on conn) is the DOMINANT pattern — DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:143-159, SettingsView.xaml.cs:87-110, FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs:653-667 and :758. Only RestructureView.xaml.cs:546 and PersonDetailSheet.xaml.cs:96 use `using var conn`. Line 94 follows the codebase's established convention, not a deviation.

2) "HOLDS A READ LOCK / DELAYS WAL CHECKPOINT" — not substantiated. The connection is ReadOnly, runs a single `SELECT COUNT(*)`, then the lambda returns. In WAL mode a reader only holds a read-mark while a statement/read-transaction is live; after ExecuteScalar() returns and `cmd` is disposed, the connection is at rest in autocommit with no open statement and no active read transaction, so it holds NO read lock while idle. A truncating checkpoint could only be momentarily blocked during the sub-millisecond COUNT read — not by the idle leaked connection. This claimed harm does not occur.

3) "ACCUMULATES OPEN FILE HANDLES UNTIL GC" — bounded and self-healing. Microsoft.Data.Sqlite 9.0.0 (pinned in Directory.Packages.props:40) owns the native sqlite3* via SqliteConnectionHandle : SafeHandle, whose finalizer calls sqlite3_close_v2, so the OS file handle IS reclaimed on GC. The real residue is a leaked pooled-connection slot, which is a minor, GC-bounded inefficiency in a low-frequency UI path (OnLoaded / toggle / post-cluster refresh), not unbounded handle growth.

NET: The missing `using` is a real, minor code-quality inconsistency (the suggested fix is reasonable and harmless), but the claimed concrete harms — read-lock contention, checkpoint delay, file-handle accumulation — are overstated or incorrect, and the "outlier vs. correct siblings" premise is factually false. No crash, hang, data loss, or wrong UI. Under the skeptical impact lens this is benign/cosmetic, so: refuted. (Confidence held below 0.9 because it is a genuine missing-`using` that a strict reviewer could legitimately flag as a low-severity leak; the refutation is about the claimed harm/severity, not the existence of the missing dispose.)

confirmed.title: OnDragOver dereferences e.DragUIOverride without a null check
confirmed.description: OnDragOver unconditionally reads e.DragUIOverride.IsCaptionVisible / IsContentVisible / IsGlyphVisible after confirming only that the data view contains StorageItems. DragEventArgs.DragUIOverride is documented to be null for drag operations that do not support custom drag-UI feedback (some non-Explorer / cross-process or programmatic drag sources). When it is null, line 652 throws NullReferenceException. The handler is a synchronous UI-thread event, so the throw is caught by App.OnUnhandledException (e.Handled = true) and the process survives, but the drag/drop overlay logic is aborted for that drag — the overlay never shows and the visual feedback for that source is broken. The sibling OnDragLeave/OnDrop paths don't touch DragUIOverride and are unaffected. Trigger: drag content over the window from a source that does not provide a DragUIOverride object.
confirmed.trigger: Drag items onto the MainWindow from a drag source that does not supply DragEventArgs.DragUIOverride (e.g. certain non-Explorer/programmatic sources); OnDragOver then NREs on e.DragUIOverride.IsCaptionVisible.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Guard the customization block: `if (e.DragUIOverride is { } ui) { ui.IsCaptionVisible = false; ui.IsContentVisible = false; ui.IsGlyphVisible = false; }` and set AcceptedOperation/DragOverlay.Visibility independently of the override being present.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against source. MainWindow.xaml.cs:645-657: OnDragOver gates only on `e.DataView.Contains(StandardDataFormats.StorageItems)` (line 647), then unconditionally dereferences `e.DragUIOverride.IsCaptionVisible/IsContentVisible/IsGlyphVisible` (lines 652-654) with no null guard. The handler is wired and reachable: MainWindow.xaml:88-91 sets AllowDrop="True" and DragOver="OnDragOver"; DragOver is a synchronous UI-thread routed event, so there is no threading/marshaling excuse that would refute it.

Premise check (the crux): WinUI 3/UWP `DragEventArgs.DragUIOverride` is a reference-typed property documented as nullable ("an object that provides custom drag UI, if there is one"), null for sources that do not supply drag-UI feedback. The common Explorer file-drag case populates it, so the trigger is narrow, but the nullable contract is real — the code does violate it without a guard. Not a misread.

Harm is real but minor and self-limiting, consistent with severity=low: App.xaml.cs:282-295 OnUnhandledException writes a dump and sets e.Handled=true, so a NRE thrown synchronously from OnDragOver is swallowed and the process survives. The NRE fires at line 652, after `e.AcceptedOperation = Copy` (649) but before `DragOverlay.Visibility = Visible` (655), so for a no-override source the overlay never shows — exactly as described. Siblings confirmed unaffected: OnDragLeave (659-662) and OnDropCoreAsync (680+) never touch DragUIOverride; PeopleView.OnClusterDragOver (366-383) doesn't either.

Genuine, triggerable (narrowly), and harmful (broken overlay feedback, no crash) — a real low-severity latent null-deref. Confidence held at 0.72 rather than higher because the null trigger is uncommon for the Explorer/StorageItems path this handler primarily targets; the suggested guard fix is correct.

Verified against MainWindow.xaml.cs:645-657. OnDragOver, wired via DragOver="OnDragOver" (MainWindow.xaml:89), is a synchronous UI-thread routed handler. After only checking e.DataView.Contains(StandardDataFormats.StorageItems) (line 647), it unconditionally dereferences e.DragUIOverride.IsCaptionVisible/IsContentVisible/IsGlyphVisible (lines 652-654) with no null guard. The dereference being unconditional is factually correct.

Null reachability: DragEventArgs.DragUIOverride is a nullable reference in the WinUI/UWP projection, and Microsoft's own docs/samples guard it (if (e.DragUIOverride != null)). It is reliably non-null for shell/Explorer file drops (the common case), but can be null for programmatic/cross-process StorageItems drag sources that provide no drag-UI customization channel. So the NRE is triggerable, though only by an atypical drag source — not by the ordinary Explorer drop.

Harm/thread reality: The claim does not assert an off-thread issue and correctly treats this as a synchronous UI-thread handler. I confirmed the recovery path: App.OnUnhandledException (App.xaml.cs:282-295) sets e.Handled = true and intentionally recovers, so the process does NOT crash. Consequence when null: the NRE skips line 655 (DragOverlay.Visibility = Visible), so the overlay/visual feedback is lost for that one drag source; re-thrown each DragOver tick. Benign, self-recovering, cosmetic — matches the claimed "low" severity.

Sibling OnClusterDragOver (PeopleView.xaml.cs:366) never touches DragUIOverride, correctly noted as unaffected. The suggested fix (guard with `if (e.DragUIOverride is { } ui)` and set AcceptedOperation/overlay independently) is correct and idiomatic. Conclusion: a genuine but very-low-severity defensive-coding gap on a nullable OS-provided property; real, triggerable (edge-case source), and the harm is accurately characterized as benign. Confidence is held below certainty only because the trigger requires an uncommon non-Explorer drag source.

Verified the cited code at platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/MainWindow.xaml.cs:645-657. OnDragOver is a synchronous void routed-event handler wired via MainWindow.xaml:88-91 (AllowDrop="True", DragOver="OnDragOver"). Inside the `e.DataView.Contains(StandardDataFormats.StorageItems)` branch, lines 652-654 do unconditionally dereference e.DragUIOverride with no null guard — the structural observation is accurate, and the sibling PeopleView.OnClusterDragOver (PeopleView.xaml.cs:366) confirms this is the only DragUIOverride site (grep found no other usages).

However the claim is refuted on both the trigger and the impact:

1) Trigger is unsubstantiated. The branch that reaches line 652 requires the drag to carry StandardDataFormats.StorageItems — which is what shell/Explorer-style sources provide, and those are exactly the sources for which the WinUI framework supplies a DragUIOverride so the app can customize caption/glyph/content. The claim's posited null case ("non-Explorer/programmatic sources that don't supply DragUIOverride") is in tension with the StorageItems precondition and is not documented by Microsoft as a nullable case, nor cited to any concrete reproducible source. A StorageItems-bearing drag over an AllowDrop target yielding a null override is not a demonstrated platform behavior.

2) Impact, even granting an exotic null, is recovered and cosmetic — failing the harm lens. App.xaml.cs:282-295 (OnUnhandledException) writes a crash dump, logs, and sets e.Handled = true (line 294), deliberately recovering UI-thread exceptions rather than terminating. So an NRE here is NOT a native fast-fail / crash / hang / leak / data-loss. The only consequence is that DragOverlay.Visibility = Visible (line 655) would not run for that one drag frame, so the custom overlay momentarily wouldn't appear; e.AcceptedOperation (line 649) was already set before the throw, and the actual drop path (OnDropCoreAsync, lines 680-710+) is fully independent of DragUIOverride, so drop functionality is intact. The next DragOver tick re-fires. This is transient, per-frame cosmetic degradation at worst.

Net: structurally a defensive-hardening nice-to-have (the suggested `if (e.DragUIOverride is { } ui)` guard is reasonable), but not a triggerable, harmful bug under the impact lens. Severity 'low' is generous; real-world harm is effectively nil because the trigger is dubious and the failure is caught and recovered.
